**Summary.** This paper reports the first demonstration of photon-pair generation via spontaneous parametric down-conversion on a thin-film lithium tantalate platform. By using periodically poled waveguides and resonators, the authors achieved high-purity single photons and high-fidelity entanglement. This establishes TFLT as a competitive material for integrated quantum photonic circuits and wavelength-multiplexed quantum communications.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum information because it establishes a new, high-performance integrated platform for generating entangled photon pairs and quantum frequency combs, essential for scalable quantum networks.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The lack of demonstrated spontaneous parametric down-conversion (SPDC) based photon-pair generation on the thin-film lithium tantalate (TFLT) platform, which is a promising candidate for scalable quantum light generation.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrated the first SPDC photon-pair generation in TFLT using both straight waveguides and racetrack resonators, achieving high-purity single photons and high-fidelity time-energy entanglement.

**Method.** The researchers used periodic poling for quasi-phase matching in TFLT, combined with electron-beam lithography and dry etching. Characterization involved SHG/SFG spectroscopy, joint spectral intensity reconstruction, and Franson-type interferometry.

**Model / system.** The experimental platform consists of periodically poled thin-film lithium tantalates (PPLT) in both traveling-wave (straight waveguides) and resonant (racetrack resonator) configurations.

**Key observables.** Photon-pair generation rate (PGR), coincidence-to-accidental ratio (CAR), heralded second-order correlation g2_H(0), Franson interference visibility, and joint spectral intensity (JSI).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Pair generation efficiency of 2.1 GHz mW^-1, CAR up to 3.8e5, g2_H(0) of 0.0018, and Franson visibility of 98.9%.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1: Fabrication process, device micrographs, and classical SHG/SFG characterization. Fig 2: Quantum characterization of waveguides including PGR and g2_H(tau). Fig 3: SHG power dependence. Fig 4: Quantum characterization of racetrack resonators.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the TFLT platform, details the fabrication and poling process, presents classical characterization of nonlinearities, demonstrates quantum photon-pair generation in waveguides, extends the demonstration to microresonators for frequency combs, and concludes with the platform's potential for quantum communications.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Chip-integrated photon-pair sources based on spontaneous parametric down-conversion (SPDC) have emerged as a promising solution for scalable quantum light generation. Thin-film lithium tantalate (TFLT) is a compelling $χ^{(2)}$ platform, combining strong nonlinearity with a high optical-damage threshold, weak photorefractive response, and ferroelectricity that enables quasi-phase matching. However, SPDC-based photon-pair generation on TFLT has not yet been demonstrated. Here, we combine high-quality periodic poling with low-loss nanophotonic waveguides to realize photon-pair sources on TFLT in both traveling-wave and resonant configurations. In periodically poled straight waveguides, we achieve broadband photon-pair generation with high efficiency ($2.1~\mathrm{GHz}~\mathrm{mW}^{-1}$) and coincidence-to-accidental ratio (up to $3.8\times10^{5}$). We further confirm high-purity single-photon operation via heralded second-order correlation ($g^{(2)}_\mathrm{H}(0) = 0.0018 \pm 0.0002$) and high-fidelity time-energy entanglement through Franson interference (visibility of $98.9 \pm 0.5\%$). In periodically poled racetrack resonators, we map out a broad quantum frequency comb spanning the telecom C- and L-bands. By isolating individual frequency-correlated pairs, we measure a high spectral brightness of $11~\mathrm{GHz}~\mathrm{mW}^{-1}~\mathrm{GHz}^{-1}$. These results are competitive with the state of the art across $χ^{(2)}$ integrated platforms, positioning TFLT as a strong contender for integrated quantum light sources, with applications in wavelength-multiplexed quantum communications and photonic quantum information processing.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2605.25406"></a>
### [Beyond Local Detailed Balance: Microscopic Rates Reshape Nonequilibrium Phase Behavior](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25406v1)

**Authors:** Takahiro Kanazawa, Kyogo Kawaguchi, Kyosuke Adachi  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.25406v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `non-equilibrium dynamics` **4/5** · `driven-dissipative phase transition` **3/5** · `non-equilibrium universality` **3/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.25406_figures/2605.25406_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1. Driven lattice gas parameterized by θ and qualitative steady-state phase diagrams. (a) We consider particles with onsite exclusion, nearest-neighbor attraction U, and external field E along the x axis. The parameter θ controls asymmetry in the hopping rate along the field direction, while preserving the same LDB. (b) Phase diagram for homogeneous states with weak attractive interaction (U = 0.5), represented by structure factor discontinuity ∆S . We also plot heatmaps of anisotropic structure factor S (k) at indicated parameter sets. (c) Phase diagram for phase-separated states with strong attractive interaction (U = 2), represented by the peak height H of structure factor, signed...</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.25406_figures/2605.25406_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 2. θ-dependent configurational biases in the two-particle system with L = 2. (a) Diagonally, horizontally, and vertically aligned con- figurations. (b) Ratio of the steady-state probabilities for vertically and horizontally aligned configurations, Pss v /Pss h [Eq. (4)]. When U = 0 or E = 0, Pss v /Pss h = 1 for any θ.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.25406_figures/2605.25406_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 3. θ-dependent anisotropy in long-range density correlation. (a) Discontinuity in the structure factor ∆S as a function of θ for various E, obtained numerically from lattice gas simulations. (b) Counterpart of (a), obtained analytically from fluctuating hydrodynamics.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.25406_figures/2605.25406_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 4. θ-dependent morphology and stability of phase separation. (a), (b) Snapshots for typical dynamics at (a) θ = −0.5 (horizontally aligned stable pattern) and (b) θ = 0.5 (vertically aligned unstable pattern). (c) Total number of clusters in the steady state as a function of θ. The brightness suggests the strength of E, and the shadow for each curve indicates the standard deviation. Inset: for E = 1, we plot the autocorrelation of large-scale patterns, Re[Ck(τ)].</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.25406_figures/2605.25406_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> FIG. 5. Relaxation dynamics of structure factors with weak attrac- tive interactions. We plot the time evolution of the long-wavelength components of the structure factor S (k) [(kx, ky) = (2π/L, 0) for (a) and (0, 2π/L) for (b)] averaged over independent samples for the pa- rameter sets used in Fig. 1(b). The gray area suggests the time points used for averaging.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** The paper shows that in nonequilibrium systems, the specific form of microscopic transition rates can control macroscopic phase behavior, such as the orientation and stability of patterns. By tuning a parameter that preserves local detailed balance, the authors demonstrate a switch in the anisotropy of long-range correlations and pattern morphology. This highlights that nonequilibrium phase behavior is more sensitive to dynamical rules than equilibrium systems.

**Why it may be interesting.** This is relevant for many-body dynamics as it demonstrates that the specific choice of microscopic dynamical rules can fundamentally alter macroscopic steady-state properties and long-range correlations, even when the thermodynamic driving force is held constant.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating whether the specific functional form of microscopic transition rates can reshape many-body phase behavior and pattern formation in strongly interacting nonequilibrium systems, even when those rates satisfy the same Local Detailed Balance condition.

**Main result.** The parameter theta, which tunes the asymmetry of hopping rates without violating LDB, can qualitatively change macroscopic phase behavior by reversing the sign of structure-factor discontinuity and switching the orientation and stability of phase-separated patterns.

**Method.** The study uses Monte Carlo simulations, a high-temperature expansion to derive effective interactions, and an approximate fluctuating hydrodynamic equation derived via a path-integral formalism.

**Model / system.** A two-dimensional driven lattice gas with hard-core exclusion and nearest-neighbor attractive interactions subject to an external driving field along the x-axis.

**Key observables.** Structure factor S(k) and its discontinuity at the origin, peak height of the structure factor, cluster analysis (number of connected components), and the autocorrelation function.

**Important parameters / regimes.** theta (asymmetry parameter), U (interaction strength), E (external field strength), and beta (inverse temperature).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The hydrodynamic derivation relies on a small wave vector expansion up to O(k^2) and assumes additive noise in the linearized regime.

**Figures summary.** Comparison of MC simulations and hydrodynamic predictions for the structure factor discontinuity, and snapshots showing stable horizontal patterns versus unstable, breaking vertical patterns.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of rate-dependence in LDB systems, describes the parameterized lattice gas model, presents analytical results for two-particle systems, details the hydrodynamic derivation and high-temperature expansion, and concludes with simulation results validating the predicted phase behavior changes.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Local detailed balance (LDB) is a central guiding principle for modeling nonequilibrium stochastic dynamics, yet it only constrains the ratio of forward and backward transition rates and does not fix the steady state. Although the functional form of rates under the same LDB has been shown to affect correlation properties in weakly interacting systems, whether it can reshape phase behavior in strongly interacting systems remains unclear. Here, for a two-dimensional driven lattice gas with attractive nearest-neighbor interactions, we consider hopping rates with a parameter that preserves the same LDB but tunes asymmetry along the driving force. We find that this parameter controls qualitative phase behavior: in the homogeneous phase, it reverses the sign of the structure-factor discontinuity and hence the anisotropy in long-range density correlations; in the phase-separated regime, it switches the orientation of anisotropic patterns and their long-time stability. Both effects are coherently captured by an approximate fluctuating hydrodynamic equation. The results demonstrate that, in contrast to equilibrium systems, nonequilibrium phase behavior depends on specific dynamical rules even when following the same LDB.

</details>

### [High fidelity preservation of photonic hyperentanglement in a free-space optical delay line](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25609v1)

**Authors:** Yu Guo, Arya Chowdhury, Pranay Tiwari, Jia Boon Chin, Anindya Banerji, Alexander Ling  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.25609v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `QC/QI experiment` **4/5** · 🔥 `quantum optics experiment` **4/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **2/5** · `interference shaping light` **2/5**

Experimental setup for generation, propagation, and characterization of hyperentangled photon pairs in a free-space opti- cal delay line (FSODL). (a) Hyperentangled photon pairs are generated via spontaneous parametric down-conversion (SPDC) in a PPKTP crystal. The signal photon propagates through the FSODL, while the idler photon serves as a reference. (b) Energy-time entanglement is characterized using an unbalanced interferometer with a piezo controlled phase ∆φ. (c) Polarization correlations are measured using polarization analyzers for the signal and idler photons. PBS: polarization beam splitter; BS: beam splitter; PP- KTP: periodically poled potassium titanyl phosphate...</figcaption>

Time-bin-resolved coincidence measurements demonstrating the preservation of energy-time entanglement after propaga- tion through the FSODL. (a) Coincidence histogram of the photons before the delay line under constructive interference, centered at zero time delay. The inset shows the corresponding histogram measured under destructive interference. (b) Coincidence histogram of the delayed photons after propagation through the delay line under constructive interference, centered at 647 ns. The shaded regions indicate the post-selection window used to extract coincidence counts for interference analysis. (c) Normalized coincidence counts as a function of the two-photon phase ∆φ for...</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.25609_figures/2605.25609_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Fig. 3. Polarization entanglement characterization after prop- agation through the optical delay line. (a) Polarization corre- lation fringes measured in the H/V and D/A bases, showing high visibility sinusoidal oscillations. (b) Real part of the re- constructed two-photon polarization density matrix for pho- tons after the delay line. The dashed boxes indicate the struc- ture of a maximally entangled polarization state. (c) Imaginary part of the reconstructed density matrix after the delay line. The dominant diagonal elements and strong coherence terms between |HH⟩and |VV⟩are consistent with the preservation of a high fidelity polarization entangled state.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that a free-space optical delay line can preserve hyperentangled photon states in both polarization and energy-time degrees of freedom for up to 647 ns. By using an all-reflective nested Herriott cell, the researchers achieved high-fidelity entanglement preservation, suggesting a scalable route for synchronization and multiplexing in quantum networks.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum networking as it provides a room-temperature, broadband alternative to cryogenic quantum memories for managing multi-DOF quantum states.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The challenge of maintaining simultaneous correlations across multiple degrees of freedom (hyperentanglement) in photon pairs for synchronization in quantum networks.

**Main result.** Demonstrated high-fidelity preservation of both polarization and energy-time entanglement after a 647 ns delay using a free-space optical delay line.

**Method.** The researchers used a nested Herriott cell for the delay and employed an unbalanced Michelson interferometer for energy-time analysis and quantum state tomography for polarization characterization.

**Model / system.** An experimental platform consisting of a type-0 PPKTP crystal pumped by a 405.2 nm laser to generate hyperentangled photon pairs at 780.0 nm and 842.6 nm, passed through an all-reflective nested Herriott cell delay line.

**Key observables.** Energy-time visibility, CHSH parameter (S), polarization state fidelity, and two-photon interference fringes.

**Important parameters / regimes.** 647 ns temporal delay, 93.9% energy-time visibility, CHSH parameter of 2.758, and a 30 THz bandwidth.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The energy-time visibility is partially limited by the intrinsic contrast of the analysis interferometer.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the experimental setup for SPDC, the delay line, and analyzers; Fig 2 displays coincidence histograms and interference fringes; Fig 3 presents polarization correlation fringes and reconstructed density matrices.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of hyperentanglement synchronization, describes the experimental setup (SPDC source and Herriott cell), details the measurement techniques for different degrees of freedom, presents the results for entanglement preservation, and concludes with implications for quantum networks.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Photonic hyperentanglement enables increased information capacity and enhanced functionality for quantum communication and networking. However, synchronization of hyperentangled photon pairs requires maintaining correlations simultaneously across multiple degrees of freedom (DOFs). The preservation of polarization and energy-time entanglement in hyperentangled photon pairs is demonstrated using a free-space optical delay line based on nested Herriott cells. After a delay of 647 ns, a two-photon interference visibility of 93.9(3)% is observed in the energy-time DOF, while a CHSH parameter of 2.758(5) is obtained in the polarization DOF. These results confirm that entanglement correlations in both DOFs are preserved after propagation through the delay line. They demonstrate that free-space optical delay lines are compatible with complex photonic quantum states and provide a promising route toward delay-based memories for synchronization and multiplexing in quantum networks.

</details>

### [Optimal Quantum Differential Privacy via Fisher Information Spectral Analysis](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24166v1)

**Authors:** Justice Owusu Agyemang, Jerry John Kponyo, Elliot Amponsah, Godfred Manu Addo Boakye  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.24166v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `QC/QI experiment` **4/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **2/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **2/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5** · `quantum measurements` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1: Quantum circuit transpiled for ibm_fez (156-qubit Eagle r3 processor) from IBM Quantum job d7s2kscf3ras73b7h4ug. The circuit encodes 4-dimensional classical data using the native gate set {RZ, SX, X, CZ, ID} at optimization level 3. Logical qubits {0, 1, 2, 3} are mapped to physical qubits {16, 21, 22, 23} on the heavy-hex topology, with CZ entangling gates placed according to the device’s nearest-neighbor connectivity. The rotation strengths α = [3.0, 1.0, 0.3, 0.1] create a 100:1 QFI eigenvalue condition number. The circuit has depth 14 with 29 operations and produces 8-bit measurement outcomes (2 classical bits per logical qubit).</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.24166_figures/2605.24166_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 3: Privacy–utility tradeoff. The optimal targeted channel (red dashed) achieves substantially lower ε than isotropic depolarizing (blue solid) at the same utility. The geometric channel (green dot-dashed, linear weights) produces very small ε values but is overly conservative.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.24166_figures/2605.24166_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Fig. 5 presents the empirical privacy–accuracy Pareto frontier. The optimal channel dominates the low-ε regime (ε ∈[2, 4)), achieving 85% ac- curacy at ε = 2.3, a privacy level isotropic DP cannot match without exceeding ε &gt; 4.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.24166_figures/2605.24166_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Fig. 7 demonstrates the QFI-aligned composition advantage. At practical noise levels (γ = 0.1), the advantage exceeds 2× by k = 20 layers and reaches 9× at k = 100. For lower noise (γ = 0.01), the crossover occurs at k = 163 lay- ers. The saturation value ε∗= (∆2/2)λmax/(cγ) decreases with increasing γ, representing the fun- damental privacy floor for infinitely deep circuits with aligned QFI structure.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.24166_figures/2605.24166_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Fig. 6 and Table 1 quantify hardware noise impact. Across four noise regimes, hardware- induced fidelity loss ranges from 0.000 (ideal) to 0.041 (high: T1 = 50 µs, T2 = 30 µs, 1Q depo- larizing 10−3, 2Q depolarizing 3 × 10−2). The loss is modest, confirming that intentional DP noise dominates privacy protection for typical QML embeddings. However, for embeddings with λmax ≲0.1, hardware noise alone provides mean- ingful privacy amplification.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a new framework for Quantum Differential Privacy that aligns noise with the geometric structure of quantum embeddings. By targeting the most sensitive directions in the Quantum Fisher Information spectrum, the method achieves much tighter privacy guarantees than standard methods. The work also reveals that certain types of hardware noise can be leveraged to amplify privacy.

**Why it may be interesting.** This is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and quantum information, as it treats hardware decoherence (dephasing) not just as an error to be corrected, but as a potential resource for enhancing privacy in quantum machine learning.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Current Quantum Differential Privacy (QDP) frameworks use isotropic noise that ignores the anisotropic geometry of quantum embeddings, leading to suboptimal privacy-utility trade-offs.

**Main result.** The authors propose a geometry-aware noise allocation strategy aligned with the Quantum Fisher Information (QIM) eigenstructure, achieving significantly tighter privacy bounds and demonstrating that hardware dephasing can act as a constructive privacy resource.

**Method.** The paper uses spectral analysis of the QFI matrix, minimax optimization via KKT conditions, and adaptive estimation using Exponential Moving Averages (EMA) to design a metric-adapted quantum channel.

**Model / system.** The study utilizes 4-qubit parameterized quantum circuits (anisotropic and isotropic embeddings) and validates results on the 156-qubit IBM Quantum 'ibm_fez' processor and Qiskit Aer GPU simulations.

**Key observables.** Privacy parameter (epsilon), Quantum Fisher Information (QFI) eigenvalues, fidelity, and adversarial mutual information.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Privacy budget (epsilon), noise strength (gamma), maximum QFI eigenvalue (lambda_max), and the misalignment angle between dephasing and measurement bases.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes a well-defined sensitivity (Delta) and specific properties of the quantum embedding manifold.

**Figures summary.** Figures demonstrate the privacy-utility tradeoff, the concentration of information leakage in the top QFI mode, the reduction of distinguishability in the minimum-QFI mode, and the impact of misaligned-basis dephasing on adversarial information.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of suboptimal isotropic noise, develops a geometric framework using QFI spectral analysis, proves six principal theorems regarding optimal noise allocation, privacy-utility relations, and composition, and validates the theory through both large-scale simulations and IBM hardware experiments.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The Quantum Fisher Information (QFI) metric governs a fundamental duality: it quantifies both how precisely a parameter can be estimated (metrology) and how distinguishable two quantum states are (privacy). We exploit this duality to establish a geometry-aware framework for quantum differential privacy (DP) that replaces isotropic depolarizing noise with direction-dependent noise aligned to the QFI eigenstructure of the quantum embedding. We prove six principal theorems: (1) the minimax-optimal mechanism concentrates the noise budget in the dominant QFI eigenmode, achieving $\varepsilon = (Δ^2/2)λ_{\max}(1-cγ)$ with $O(d/λ_{\max})$ advantage; (2) mixed-state QFI decomposition reveals that dephasing in the adversary's basis $\textit{increases}$ accessible information, while misaligned-basis dephasing provides constructive privacy amplification from hardware noise; (3) a tight privacy $-$ utility uncertainty relation $\varepsilon \cdot (1 - F) \ge \frac{Δ^2}{2}\frac{\operatorname{Tr}(F)}{d}$; (4) adaptive QFI estimation converging at $O(1/\sqrt{n})$ yields $1.92\times$ tighter bounds; (5) QFI-aligned composition saturates at $O(1)$ versus $O(k)$ for standard composition; and (6) hardware noise can be harnessed for privacy amplification. Adversarial vulnerabilities, Wasserstein guarantees, subspace projection, and a zero-knowledge audit protocol follow as corollaries. Results are validated on Qiskit Aer GPU simulations, IBM Quantum hardware (ibm_fez, 156 qubits), and against classical DP baselines, achieving equivalent utility at $\varepsilon \approx 0.001$ versus $\varepsilon \approx 4800$ for classical DP.

</details>

### [Thermalization in Spatially Extended Open Quantum Systems: Local versus Global Markovian Evolution](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25760v1)

**Authors:** Jorge Tabanera-Bravo, Massimiliano Esposito, Felipe Barra, Juan M. R. Parrondo  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.25760v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `non-equilibrium dynamics` **4/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **3/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **3/5** · `scars & prethermalization` **2/5**

(b,c) Evolution of the eigensate populations ρii for ϵ = 0.1 in (b) and ϵ = 5 × 10−5 in (c). The two dashed gray lines give the value of the ratio for the global and local thermal states ρtherm, given by Eq. (72), and ρloc, given by (73), respectively. Other parameters: β = m = 0.1, g = 50, Γ = 1, σp...</figcaption>
</figure>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 4: Populations of the eigenstates of the chain Hamilto- nian as given by the exact map Eq. (38) (bottom panel) and the local limit Eqs. (54)–(56) (top panel). Observe that, in the local limit, the energy levels within the two bands do not thermalize, as expected. In this figure Γ = 1, with ϵ = 0.1. Other parameters are β = m = 0.1, g = 50, σp = 0.5 and h = 4.</figcaption>
</figure>

<img src="2605.25760_figures/2605.25760_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 3: The two plots depict the evolution of the inter-band temperature βeff(0 →2) and the intra-band coherence ρ23 (insets) for ϵ = 0.1 (top) and 3×10−3 (bottom). We show the results for the exact evolution, given by the map (38) (blue), the local limit which neglects terms of order Γϵ, Eqs. (54)–(56) (orange), and the global approximation based on wave packets with negligible momentum dispersion, Eq. (45) (black). The grey line indicates the temperature of the reservoir, which is β = 0.1. Other parameters are m = 0.1, g = 50, Γ = 1, σp = 0.5 and h = 4.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper resolves the discrepancy between local and global Lindblad equations in extended quantum systems by using a collisional model. It demonstrates that a specific master equation can achieve both thermodynamic consistency and local dynamics, depending on the momentum dispersion of the bath. This work is significant for understanding how thermalization emerges in many-body systems.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a resolution to a fundamental conflict in open quantum systems theory, showing how a single framework can bridge the gap between local dissipative models and global secular approximations while preserving thermodynamic consistency.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating the tension between local and global Markovian evolution in spatially extended open quantum systems, specifically addressing how to maintain thermodynamic consistency while capturing dynamical locality.

**Main result.** The authors derive a Lindblad equation from a collisional model that is both thermodynamically consistent (driving the system to a Gibbs state) and local at short times, identifying a crossover regime governed by the non-commuting limits of momentum dispersion and inter-qubit coupling.

**Method.** The study uses a collision model with repeated interactions between a qubit chain and a thermal reservoir, employing quantum scattering theory, Kraus representations, and perturbative analysis of the master equation.

**Model / system.** A spatially extended chain of N interacting qubits (e.g., an XX-model type Hamiltonian) coupled locally to a thermal reservoir via collisions with particles described by wave packets with specific momentum dispersion.

**Key observables.** Populations of energy eigenstates, coherences in the energy eigenbasis, and the stationary thermal state.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Inter-qubit coupling strength (epsilon), momentum dispersion of the bath (sigma_p), dissipation rate (Gamma), and inverse temperature (beta).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The Hamiltonian is assumed to have non-degenerate energy levels, the system is in the weak-coupling regime, and the reservoir interaction is localized at the first qubit.

**Figures summary.** A sketch of the transition processes (collisions vs. Hamiltonian evolution), evolution of eigenstate populations for different coupling strengths, and comparisons between local and global population dynamics.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the conflict between local and global dynamics, defines the collisional model and scattering framework, derives the master equations, analyzes the non-commuting limits of coupling and dispersion, and provides numerical verification using a 3-qubit chain.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate the dynamics of a qubit chain locally coupled to a thermal reservoir, modeled through repeated collisions with particles drawn from a heat bath. Under suitable conditions, the resulting Lindblad equation is thermodynamically consistent -- it drives the system toward thermal equilibrium -- while remaining local at short times. This framework reveals a crossover between the global Lindblad equation derived from the secular approximation in weak-coupling theory and the local dissipative models often employed in the literature, which generally fail to ensure thermodynamic consistency.

</details>

**Authors:** Syo Kamata  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.24555v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 3: Complex-plane trajectories of the ODSD F(O)(p) for p = 0 and p = 1 under the variation of the bare energy E1 ∈[0.02, 2.02]. We compare the total qubit population Oqub with the population difference Odiff. The continuous deformation of these loops under drive-induced dressing highlights the onset of severe destructive interference, serving as the geometric precursor to the quantized visibility jumps shown in Fig. 4.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.24555_figures/2605.24555_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 4: (Left) Drive-induced redistribution of the mode-wise phase accumulation observed by Oqub as a function of the drive amplitude A. (Right) The visibility loss ∆(O) vis (p) for p = 0, 1 evaluated for the total population Oqub and the difference observable Odiff. The sharp, quantized steps in the visibility loss reflect winding-changing events where the continuous deformation of the dressing coefficients forces the complex trajectory of F(O)(p) to cross the origin, abruptly shedding a full 2π phase rotation.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.24555_figures/2605.24555_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> FIG. 5: Sampled observable dimension Dobs = rank(K0) in the DTQ3 model plotted against the number of micromotion slices Q. In the isolated qubit limit (B = 0), the observable remains trapped within a one-dimensional invariant sector (Dobs = 1). In contrast, turning on coherent leakage (B = 0.47) dynamically accesses the third level, triggering an algebraic expansion of the visible operator space that reaches the full qutrit operator-space dimension N 2 = 9.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a method called Floquet algebraic tomography to reconstruct the underlying dynamics of non-Hermitian periodic systems from measurement traces. It distinguishes between the system's intrinsic spectrum and the effects of the chosen measurement probe, providing a way to identify spectral features like Exceptional Points even when parts of the system are hidden by symmetry.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a rigorous algebraic toolset for characterizing the dynamics of open, driven quantum systems (like transmons or topological chains) where only partial, stroboscopic measurements are available, specifically addressing the complex physics of Exceptional Points.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Developing an algebraic framework to reconstruct the spectral properties and monodromy matrix of non-Hermitian Floquet systems from limited, time-discrete observable trace data.

**Main result.** The authors establish a 'Floquet algebraic tomography' framework that separates intrinsic spectral features from observable-dependent dressing, proving that while certain parts of the dynamics may remain invisible due to symmetries, the 'visible' part of the monodromy matrix can be reconstructed using Cayley-Hamilton and Hankel methods.

**Method.** The framework utilizes Observable Trace Sequences (OTS), the Observable Resolvent, and Cayley-Hamilton recurrences, alongside Hankel matrix constructions and Prony-type methods for spectral reconstruction.

**Model / system.** Finite-dimensional non-Hermitian Floquet systems characterized by a monodromy matrix M in GL(N, C), with specific applications to a driven transmon qutrit and a finite non-Hermitian Floquet SSH chain.

**Key observables.** Observable Trace Sequence (OTS) defined as Tr(OM^n), observable resolvent, spectral determinant, and Dirichlet spectral data.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Spectral regimes including Simple Spectra (SS), Diabolic Points (DP), and Exceptional Points (EP); observable dimension and algebraic deficiency.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The system is finite-dimensional, the monodromy matrix is assumed to be stationary/time-periodic, and the observable O is independent of the discrete period index n.

**Figures summary.** Figure 9 demonstrates the growth of observable dimension as a function of micromotion slices, comparing clean, bond-disordered, and on-site-disordered configurations in an SSH chain.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the inverse problem of reconstruction, develops the algebraic framework (resolvent, determinant, and Dirichlet data), provides the reconstruction engine (Cayley-Hamilton and Hankel methods), addresses identifiability and symmetry-induced invisibility, and validates the theory with transmon and SSH chain examples.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We formulate a framework of Floquet algebraic tomography for finite-dimensional non-Hermitian monodromy matrices from observable trace sequences $ζ_n^{(O)}={\rm Tr}(OM^n)$. Since these sequences are constrained by the characteristic polynomial of $M$, the inverse problem is a finite-dimensional algebraic reconstruction problem rather than a generic exponential fit. We organize the reconstruction through the observable resolvent, spectral determinant, and Dirichlet spectral data, separating the common spectral skeleton from observable-dependent dressing. Cayley--Hamilton and Hankel methods recover the similarity-invariant spectral data, while multi-observable and Liouville-space extensions connect the construction to realization theory and tomography reconstruction. We further clarify the limits of identifiability from restricted observable algebras: the data determine a visible representative, micromotion can enlarge the sampled visible operator space, and exact symmetries impose residual invisible sectors. Two examples, a driven transmon qutrit and a finite non-Hermitian Floquet SSH chain, demonstrate leakage-induced visibility expansion, observable-dependent phase response, EP-accessible branch geometry, and disorder/probe-dependent observable-dimension readouts.

</details>

**Authors:** Jannik H. Schwarberg, Fabian Magerl, Susanne Beuer, Alexander May, Christian Gobert, Martin Siebert, Christian Miersch, Heino Möller, Wolfgang Knolle, Chihang Luo, Jan F. Dick, Franziska C. Beyer, Mathias Rommel, Jörg Schulze

**Summary.** This paper investigates how different semiconductor fabrication techniques impact the background noise of silicon vacancy emitters in 4H-SiC. By replacing plasma-based deposition with thermal oxidation and using atomic layer etching to heal surface damage, the authors significantly enhance the signal-to-noise ratio of the color centers. This progress is vital for integrating high-quality quantum emitters into mature, CMOS-compatible semiconductor platforms.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum information because it provides a practical roadmap for improving the spectral purity and signal strength of solid-state single-photon sources, which is essential for scalable quantum networks.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The background photoluminescence (PL) noise caused by conventional CMOS-compatible processing (e.g., plasma etching and PECVD deposition) masks the signal from silicon vacancy (V_Si) color centers in 4H-SiC, degrading their utility for quantum technologies.

**Main result.** The study demonstrates that using thermally grown oxides with NO annealing and combining RIE with ALE significantly reduces surface noise, achieving a signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) of up to 50 for deeper emitters and maintaining high electrical performance in lateral pin-diodes.

**Method.** The researchers compared various surface treatments (PECVD, ALD, thermal oxidation, RIE, ALE, and TMAH etching) using depth-resolved confocal PL spectroscopy, in-line wafer-scale PL mapping, and electrical I-V characterization.

**Model / system.** The experimental platform consists of 4H-SiC lateral pin-diodes on both c-plane and a-plane substrates, featuring silicon vacancy (V_Si) color centers generated via electron irradiation.

**Key observables.** Photoluminescence (PL) intensity, signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), second-order correlation function g(2), blocking voltage, and leakage current.

**Important parameters / regimes.** V_Si emission intensity (~8 kcounts/s), blocking voltage (up to 150 V), leakage current (< 10 pA/µm), and 730 nm excitation wavelength.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The paraxial approximation used in depth scans may slightly overestimate focal depth due to spherical aberration and high-angle refraction.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows PL noise in standard CMOS processes; Figure 2 uses violin plots to compare various passivation and etching techniques; Figure 3 illustrates PL trends across different processing steps; Figure 4 presents the schematic of pin-diode designs and depth scans; Figure SI 2 compares surface damage between RIE, ALE, and hybrid RIE+ALE processes.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of processing-induced noise, details the various surface treatment and fabrication strategies evaluated, presents the comparative results for optical and electrical performance, and concludes with recommendations for optimizing quantum-grade SiC devices.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Silicon vacancies ($V_\mathrm{Si}$) in 4H-SiC are promising candidates for quantum technologies due to their long spin coherence times and integrability into mature semiconductor platforms. However, conventional CMOS-compatible processing introduces significant photoluminescence noise from passivation layers and crystal damage, degrading color center coherence and excitation linewidths. This work evaluates strategies to minimize such background noise. Thermally grown oxides with nitrogen monoxide annealing provide excellent low-noise passivation, remaining stable during subsequent $600\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ thermal treatments. Furthermore, combining reactive ion etching with atomic layer etching eliminates ion-induced surface damage. Into lateral pin-diodes, used for stark shift and photoluminescent excitation linewidth tuning, a selectively etched optical window is integrated. These devices show ideal electrical properties -- blocking up to $150\,\mathrm{V}$ with leakage current below $10\,\mathrm{pA}/μ\mathrm{m}$ -- while significantly enhancing the $V_\mathrm{Si}$ environment. Single emitters in these pin-diodes show an increased signal-to-noise ratio of 15 for near-surface and of 50 for deeper emitters on both c-plane and a-plane wafers.

</details>

### [Non-monotonic evolution of multipartite entanglement under the Unruh effect](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24424v1)

**Authors:** Shu-Min Wu, Si-Han Shang, Si-Yu Liu, Rui-Yang Xu, Qianqian Liu, Xiao-Li Huang  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.24424v1>

**Summary.** This paper shows that the Unruh effect can non-monotonically evolve and even enhance multipartite entanglement in Dicke states. It demonstrates that Dicke states are more robust against Unruh-induced decoherence than GHZ or W states, making them superior resources for quantum information tasks in relativistic settings.

**Why it may be interesting.** It challenges the conventional view of entanglement degradation and provides a mechanism (mode mixing) for entanglement enhancement, which is highly relevant for understanding decoherence and resource management in relativistic quantum information and open quantum systems.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates whether multipartite entanglement in Dicke states undergoes monotonic degradation under the Unruh effect and explores the robustness of these states compared to GHZ and W states.

**Main result.** The researchers found that tetrapartite entanglement exhibits a non-monotonic evolution, initially decreasing and then increasing toward a finite value, demonstrating that the Unruh effect can actually enhance multipartite entanglement in certain regimes.

**Method.** The study employs the Unruh-DeWitt detector model within the weak-coupling regime, using first-order perturbation theory and Bogoliubov transformations to calculate the time-evolved density matrix.

**Model / system.** A four-qubit Dicke state shared among four observers, where one detector (David) undergoes uniform acceleration in a massless scalar field in Minkowski spacetime while the others remain inertial.

**Key observables.** Tetrapartite entanglement (pi4-tangle), 1-3 bipartite entanglement (negativity), and 1-1 bipartite entanglement.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Acceleration parameter (q), detector energy gap (Omega), interaction time (Delta), and effective coupling strength (nu).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The detectors are modeled as two-level systems, the interaction is localized via a Gaussian-profile smearing function, and the system is treated within the weak-coupling approximation.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the experimental setup; Figure 2 illustrates the divergence in bipartite entanglement behavior between inertial and accelerated observers; Figure 3 and 4 demonstrate the non-monotonic 'dip and recovery' of the pi4-tangle as a function of acceleration, energy gap, and interaction time.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of entanglement in non-inertial frames, describes the Unruh-DeWitt model and mathematical framework, presents results on bipartite and multipartite entanglement evolution, compares Dicke states to GHZ/W states, and discusses implications for relativistic quantum information.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate the behavior of tetrapartite entanglement in a four-qubit Dicke state under relativistic motion by employing the Unruh-DeWitt detector model, where one detector undergoes uniform acceleration. We show that the entanglement exhibits a non-monotonic evolution: it first decreases and subsequently increases toward a finite value as the acceleration grows. In contrast to the conventional view that the Unruh effect leads to a monotonic degradation of multipartite entanglement, our results demonstrate that it can instead enhance multipartite entanglement within a finite parameter regime. This behavior reveals a dual role of the Unruh effect in multipartite systems. Our findings therefore provide a refined understanding of relativistic multipartite quantum correlations, indicating that Dicke states constitute more robust multipartite quantum resources against Unruh-induced decoherence and may offer advantages for relativistic quantum information processing tasks.

</details>

### [A DMFT approach to evaluate electronic frictional effects near solid surfaces of strongly correlated systems](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25320v1)

**Authors:** Yunhao Liu, Wenjie Dou  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.25320v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `Kondo & dissipative impurity` **4/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **3/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **2/5**

Only a single peak occurs in the electronic friction. (b) Electronic friction obtained from MFT and DMFT calculations at temperature T = 0.1. Due to electron-electron repulsions in the Hubbard model, DMFT successfully captures two peaks in the friction, whereas MFT fails to reproduce them. (c) Electronic friction of the non-interacting lattice model and the Hubbard-Holstein model obtained from MFT at temperature T = 0.1. Within MFT, the friction merely corresponds to a broadening of peak without el-el interactions, indicating that it still retains many single-particle features. (d)...</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.25320_figures/2605.25320_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 2. (a) PMF and diabatic PESs as a function of position x. (b) and (c) Electronic friction and PMF as a function of position x according to MFT, respectively. (d) and (e) Corresponding results obtained from DMFT. The parameters are g = 0.075, ω = 0.1, t = 0.25, U = 1, Ed = g2</figcaption>
</figure>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 3. The results from EF-LD. (a) average kinetic energy; (b) electronic population in the impurity. The parameters are g = 0.075, ω = 0.1, t = 0.25, U = 1, Ed = g2</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that Dynamical Mean-Field Theory is essential for accurately describing electronic friction and nonadiabatic dynamics at the interface of strongly correlated materials. It shows that mean-field approaches fail to capture critical electron-attachment resonances that emerge from electron-electron interactions. The findings have significant implications for understanding energy dissipation in molecular electronics and electrochemistry.

**Why it may be interesting.** The study provides deep insights into how strong electron-electron correlations and many-body resonances (like the Kondo effect) fundamentally alter energy dissipation and nonadiabatic dynamics in interfacial systems.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Evaluating electronic friction effects and nonadiabatic dynamics near the surfaces of strongly correlated systems, specifically addressing the failure of mean-field theories to capture many-body resonances.

**Main result.** DMFT predicts two distinct peaks in electronic friction due to electron attachment/detachment resonances in the presence of electron-electron interactions, whereas MFT fails to capture these features and incorrectly predicts electronic population evolution.

**Method.** The authors employ Dynamical Mean-Field Theory (DMFT) with the Full Density-Matrix Numerical Renormalization Group (FDM-NRG) as an impurity solver, combined with Electronic Friction-Langevin Dynamics (EF-LD) simulations.

**Model / system.** A two-dimensional Hubbard-Holstein model representing a molecule (impurity) interacting with a solid surface, including electron-electron interaction (U) and electron-phonon coupling (g).

**Key observables.** Electronic friction tensor, Potential of Mean Force (PMF), electronic population (N), and average kinetic energy (Ek).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Electron-electron interaction strength (U), electron-phonon coupling (g), hopping (t), and temperature (T).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The lattice self-energy is assumed to be local (DMFT approximation), the lattice is treated as a Bethe lattice, and the electronic bath is treated as a fast-relaxing reservoir (Markovian approximation).

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows electronic friction vs. position for U=0 at various temperatures; Figure 2 compares PMF and friction coefficients for MFT vs. DMFT; Figure 3 shows the time evolution of average kinetic energy and electronic population.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of nonadiabaticity at interfaces, defines the 2D Hubbard-Holstein model, details the DMFT/FDM-NRG methodology, presents results for non-interacting and interacting regimes, compares MFT and DMFT dynamics, and concludes on the necessity of DMFT for correlated systems.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Electronic friction-Langevin dynamics (EF-LD) provides an efficient framework for capturing nonadiabatic effects at solid surfaces, with particular relevance to electrochemistry and molecular electronics. In this work, we investigate electronic friction in the two-dimensional Hubbard-Holstein model employing dynamical mean-field theory (DMFT), where the full density-matrix numerical renormalization group (FDM-NRG) serves as the impurity solver. Our results are benchmarked against mean-field theory (MFT). DMFT yields two distinct peaks in the electronic friction, arising from electron attachment/detachment resonances with the solid Fermi level, whereas MFT is unable to capture this Fermi resonance. We further examine the dynamics of electronic friction via EF-LD simulations. Our simulations uncover significant discrepancies mainly in the electronic population evolution predicted by MFT versus DMFT, indicating that MFT is inadequate for describing nonadiabatic dynamics in strongly correlated systems. Thanks to its flexibility and computational efficiency, the proposed DMFT-based approach can be readily extended to a broad range of applications.

</details>

### [Anderson Localization: A Floquet operator Krylov space perspective](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24115v1)

**Authors:** Hsiu-Chung Yeh, Aditi Mitra  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.24115v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `non-equilibrium dynamics` **4/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **3/5** · `scars & prethermalization` **2/5**

The relative standard deviation of the autocorrelation also saturates at late times (middle panel). The disorder averaged cosine of the Krylov angles is shown in the right panel, where the odd/even angles approach π/2 from above/below. Bottom panels: log–log plots of the averaged cosines of the odd (left panel) and even (middle panel) Krylov angles. For strong disorder, ⟨cos θ2j−1⟩∼1/√j, while |⟨cos θ2j⟩| ∼1/j for all...</figcaption>
</figure>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 2. The Krylov angles are computed from the disorder-averaged autocorrelation (upper left panel of Fig. 1) and shown in the left panel. In contrast to the results in Fig. 1, both the odd and even Krylov angles exhibit the same asymptotic power-law behavior, | cos θj| ∼1/j for large j (middle panel). Consequently, the trajectory of the Krylov angles in the phase diagram (right panel) lies along the diagonal. These results are consistent with the analytic solution for a constant autocorrelation which suggests a power-law localized 0-mode in Krylov space [10].</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.24115_figures/2605.24115_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">

Top panels: Bernstein–Szeg˝o approximation (13) applied to a single realization (left panel) and to the average over 15,000 realizations of the cosine of the Krylov angles (right panel). Bottom panels: Approximate spectral function obtained from the Bernstein–Szeg˝o approximation (13) based on Krylov angles extracted from the disorder-averaged autocorrelation (left panel) ; truncated Fourier transform (16) computed from the disorder-averaged autocorrelation is evaluated only at (2n∗+ 1) discrete values of the frequencies: ω = 2mπ/(2n∗+ 1) with m = −n∗,...</figcaption>
</figure>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 4. Comparison of different methods for extracting the disorder-averaged spectral function. The bottom two panels of Fig. 3, corresponding to the Bernstein–Szeg˝o approxima- tion with k∗= 40 (Φ40) and the truncated Fourier transform with n∗= 40 (Φ(40)), are plotted together with the results obtained from the Lehmann representation (34), the latter averaged over 5000 realizations. All three methods show good agreement, while the Bernstein–Szeg˝o approximation more ef- ficiently captures the delta-function peak at ω = 0.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">

Top panels: autocorrelation (left), and the corresponding Krylov angles (right). Bottom panels: PDF of the autocorrelation (left) and the corresponding CDF (right). Both the PDF and CDF are computed from the first 200 time steps of the autocorrelation.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper develops a Krylov space-based approach to study Anderson localization by treating continuous-time dynamics as a discrete-time Floquet problem. It introduces an efficient moment method for computing spectral functions and identifies clear numerical signatures for the localization-delocalization transition. The work provides a powerful tool for analyzing the long-time dynamics of disordered and quasi-periodic systems.

**Why it may be interesting.** This is highly relevant for many-body dynamics as it provides a new numerical framework for studying operator spreading and spectral properties in Floquet systems, which is a central theme in studying periodically driven quantum systems.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating Anderson localization and the localization-delocalization transition in the Aubry-Andre model using an operator Krylov space perspective.

**Main result.** The study demonstrates that mapping continuous-time dynamics to a stroboscopic Floquet problem allows for efficient spectral reconstruction and identifies specific signatures of localization, such as the absence of a Porter-Thomas distribution and power-law decay of Krylov angles.

**Method.** The authors employ operator Krylov space methods, specifically the Arnoldi iteration and a more efficient 'moment method' to extract Krylov parameters from the discrete-time autocorrelation function.

**Model / system.** The primary models are the 1D Anderson model (disordered) and the Aubary-Andre model (quasi-periodic), mapped to an effective 1D Floquet transverse field Ising model.

**Key observables.** Autocorrelation function, Krylov angles (theta_j), spectral function, Inverse Participation Ratio (IPR), and Porter-Thomas distribution.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Disorder strength (h), stroboscopic time step (T), and system size (L).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study focuses on non-interacting fermionic systems and assumes the validity of the stroboscopic approximation for studying long-time dynamics.

**Figures summary.** Figures show phase diagrams of the Floquet Ising model, disorder-averaged autocorrelation saturation, power-law decay of Krylov angles, and comparisons of different spectral reconstruction schemes.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the Krylov space mapping, compares numerical algorithms (Gram-Schmidt vs. Moment method), analyzes the localization-delocalization transition in the Anderson and Aubry-Andre models, and discusses the statistical properties of Krylov parameters and spectral functions.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The problem of Anderson localization, as well as the single particle localization-delocalizaton transition of the Aubry-André model, is studied employing operator Krylov space methods. It is shown that even when the dynamics is generated by a Hamiltonian, studying the dynamics at stroboscopic rather than continuous times has its advantages. In particular, mapping the dynamics to an effective Floquet problem results in an operator Krylov space description where quantities such as the spectral function can be computed with fewer computational resources, while a moment method exists that allows for the extraction of Krylov parameters directly from the discrete time autocorrelation function. For stroboscopic dynamics, the operator Krylov space corresponds to the dynamics of an edge operator of an inhomogeneous Floquet transverse field Ising model, with the parameters of this effective model generated recursively. The Krylov parameters show disorder-averaged renormalization with their distribution narrowing as the recursion step increases. It is shown that a more physical spectral function is obtained from the Krylov parameters obtained from the disorder-averaged autocorrelation function, rather than the disorder-averaged Krylov parameters. The delocalized (localized) phase is shown to correspond to the appearance (absence) of a Porter-Thomas distribution, a ballistically propagating (localized) wavefront in operator Krylov space, and a smooth (discrete) Berstein-Szegö power-spectrum. The localization-delocalization transition is also demonstrated in operator Krylov space. A Porter-Thomas distribution is also observed at the critical point. The long-time dynamics and the inverse participation ratio at the critical point is shown to exhibit behavior consistent with a multi-fractal scaling with system size.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.24478"></a>
### [Quantum Dynamics of Interacting dissipative oscillators: A novel scheme](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24478v1)

**Authors:** Ronak Moradi, Fardin Kheirandish  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.24478v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `non-equilibrium dynamics` **4/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **3/5** · `driven-dissipative phase transition` **2/5**

**Summary.** The paper develops a theoretical scheme to study how energy moves through a chain of interacting, dissipative oscillators under external driving. It reveals that resonance can prevent energy from growing without bound and that certain configurations can shield middle oscillators from energy increases. This work is significant for understanding energy transfer and localization in driven-dissipative quantum networks.

**Why it may be interesting.** This paper provides analytical insights into energy localization and unbounded energy gain in driven-dissipative networks, which is highly relevant to the study of open quantum systems and quantum transport.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating the quantum dynamics, energy transfer, and energy localization in networks of interacting, dissipative quantum harmonic oscillators driven by an external classical field.

**Main result.** The study finds that in the off-resonance regime, the energy of the second oscillator grows unboundedly, whereas on resonance, it remains bounded and periodic; additionally, for a three-oscillator system, the middle oscillator exhibits an energy shielding effect.

**Method.** The authors use successive Bogoliubov transformations to diagonalize the Hamiltonian and solve the Heisenberg equations of motion, supplemented by phase-space analysis using Husimi Q-functions.

**Model / system.** A system of n interacting quantum harmonic oscillators coupled to thermal reservoirs and driven by an external classical force with frequency omega_L. The Hamiltonian includes nearest-neighbor interactions and time-dependent coupling to the bath.

**Key observables.** Oscillator energies, Husimi Q-functions, reduced Husimi functions, and reduced density matrix components in the number state basis.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Resonance vs. off-resonance (detuning), temperature (zero temperature vs. thermal reservoirs), and coupling strength g(t).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The model assumes Markovian evolution and specific initial states such as separable coherent and number states.

**Figures summary.** Figure 2 shows energy growth for different detunings; Figure 3 tracks the path of the Husimi function maximum; Figure 4 compares Husimi functions at different times; Figure 5 provides a schematic of the n-oscillator system.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem, defines the Hamiltonian for two and n oscillators, applies Bogoliubov transformations to solve the dynamics, analyzes energy and phase-space evolution, and provides detailed mathematical derivations in the appendices.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate the dynamics of interacting quantum harmonic oscillators coupled to thermal reservoirs under the influence of an external driving field. In a novel theoretical scheme, we first analyze the case of two interacting oscillators, each coupled to its own thermal reservoir, with an external source applied to the first oscillator. By diagonalizing the total Hamiltonian through successive Bogoliubov transformations, we obtain explicit expressions for the oscillator energies. A key finding is that off resonance, the energy of the second oscillator grows unboundedly, whereas on resonance, it remains bounded and periodic. We then derive the Husimi functions for both oscillators and the reduced Husimi functions for initially separable coherent and number states. The shift of the Husimi function's maximum away from the origin at zero temperature confirms continuous energy gain under specific parameter conditions. An explicit formula for the reduced density matrix components in the number state basis is also provided. Finally, we generalize the model to $n$ interacting oscillators in a thermal medium driven by a classical field. For the three-oscillator case, we show that on resonance, the energies of the first and third oscillators grow, while the energy of the second oscillator remains periodically bounded. These results offer insights into energy transfer and localization in coupled oscillator systems in thermal environments.

</details>

### [Self-Generated Chiral Rotation in Whispering-Gallery Optomechanics](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24185v1)

**Authors:** Mohamed Hatifi  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.24185v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `driven-dissipative phase transition` **4/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **3/5** · `quantum measurements` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.24185_figures/2605.24185_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1. Schematic of angular-recoil backaction in a whispering-gallery-mode resonator. A localized movable scatterer at angular coordinate ϕ couples the two counterpropagating WGM modes a+ and a−, which are driven reciprocally with equal in- puts in amplitude |S+| = |S−|. A circulation-changing scattering event transfers angular momentum to the scatterer: a+ →a−gives ∆Lϕ = +2mℏ, while a−→a+ gives ∆Lϕ = −2mℏ. If the scatterer rotates with angular velocity Ω, the two scattering channels acquire opposite rotational Doppler shifts, ∆∓2mΩ. This Doppler imbalance changes the two recoil rates, producing a net torque on the scatterer. Above threshold, the reciprocal state Ω= 0 becomes unstable...</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.24185_figures/2605.24185_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">

(a) Reciprocal-drive recoil torque τrec(Ω), normalized by Γϕγ/(2m), as a function of the normalized rota- tional Doppler shift 2mΩ/γ, compared with the mechanical damp- ing torque ΓϕΩ. For n0 &lt; nth the only stable solution is Ω= 0, whereas for n0 &gt; nth two finite intersections Ω= ±Ω∗appear. Parameters in (a)–(c) are ∆/γ = 1/ √</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.24185_figures/2605.24185_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Figure 3. Optical readout of the mechanically chiral state. (a) Normalized backscattered spectra R+(ω) and R−(ω), for the two probe directions in the rotating phase.The selected angular velocity Ω∗Doppler-shifts the two backscattering channels in opposite directions, produc- ing resonances centered at detunings separated by 4mΩ∗. The plotted case has 2mΩ∗/γ = 0.84. (b) Directional backscattering asymmetry AR(ω) = [R+(ω) −R−(ω)]/[R+(ω) + R−(ω)] defined in Eq. (21). Below threshold, Ω∗= 0, and the asymmetry vanishes. Above threshold, the Doppler-split spectra produce an odd asymmetry in the probe detuning ∆p. (c) Maximum asymmetry maxω |AR| as a function of normalized photon number n0/nth....</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper shows how a movable scatterer in a whispering-gallery-mode resonator can spontaneously start rotating due to the recoil from backscattered photons. This process breaks the symmetry of the system, turning a passive mode-splitting effect into an active, self-generated chiral state. The resulting rotation can be detected through Doppler-split optical spectra.

**Why it may be interesting.** It presents a minimal mechanism for autonomous symmetry breaking and chiral optomechanics, which is highly relevant to researchers working on non-equilibrium dynamics and open quantum systems.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating whether reciprocal optical driving in a whispering-gallery-mode resonator can induce spontaneous mechanical rotation (chiral state) without externally imposed handedness.

**Main result.** The study demonstrates that angular-recoil backaction from photon backscattering can create negative angular friction, leading to a symmetry-breaking pitchfork bifurcation into two steady-state rotations.

**Method.** The author uses a weak-scattering, adiabatic, driven-dissipative model based on Langevin/input-output equations and local stability analysis of the angular dynamics.

**Model / system.** A whispering-gallery-mode (WGM) resonator containing a localized, movable scatterer (an angular degree of freedom) subject to reciprocal bidirectional optical pumping.

**Key observables.** Time-averaged optical torque, steady-state angular velocity, backscattered spectra, and backscattering asymmetry.

**Important parameters / regimes.** WGM azimuthal index (m), optical detuning (Delta), backscattering rate (J), optical linewidth (gamma), and photon number (n0).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Weak-scattering limit (J << gamma), adiabatic regime, neglect of static pinning, and neglect of one-sided propulsion or external rotation.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a schematic of angular-recoil backaction; Figure 2 plots reciprocal torque vs. Doppler shift, steady-state angular velocity vs. photon number, and time-domain evolution.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the mechanism of angular-recoil backaction, presents a mathematical model of the WGM resonator, performs stability analysis to identify the bifurcation, and concludes with the optical readout of the chiral state.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Backscattering in whispering-gallery-mode resonators is usually a passive mode-splitting mechanism produced by a fixed defect. Here, we show that, when the backscatterer is a mechanical angular degree of freedom, the same process becomes an angular-recoil backaction channel capable of generating chirality under reciprocal driving. A localized movable scatterer coherently converts photons between clockwise and counterclockwise whispering-gallery modes, transferring angular recoil in each circulation-changing event. In a weak-scattering driven-dissipative model, reciprocal bidirectional pumping gives zero net torque at rest, but rotation Doppler-shifts the two opposite scattering rates in opposite directions. For suitable detuning, this feedback produces negative angular friction, destabilizes the nonrotating reciprocal state, and selects one of two symmetry-related steady rotations. The threshold scales inversely with the square of the WGM azimuthal index. The mechanically chiral state produces a direction-dependent weak-probe response, visible as a Doppler splitting of the backscattered spectra, turning passive WGM mode splitting into a minimal mechanism for autonomous chiral optomechanics.

</details>

Panel: (a) 𝑘𝜃= 0 and (b) 𝑘𝜃= 104.</figcaption>

**Summary.** This paper shows that strong thermalization (strong ETH) can occur in quantum systems even when the underlying classical dynamics are non-chaotic, provided they are mean-ergodic. By studying the Double Kicked Top model, the authors prove that ergodicity alone, rather than chaos, is sufficient to drive the emergence of Gaussian matrix element statistics and $D^{-1/2}$ scaling.

**Why it may be interesting.** This is highly relevant for many-body dynamics as it identifies a mechanism for 'chaos-free thermalization,' which could potentially serve as a resource for quantum computing by generating entanglement without the destructive effects of quantum chaos.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates whether the strong Eigenstate Thermalization Hypothesis (ETH) can emerge from classical non-chaotic but mean-ergodic dynamics, addressing whether strong ETH is a quantum analog of ergodicity rather than specifically a consequence of chaos.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that a non-chaotic, mean-ergodic regime of the Double Kicked Top model exhibits strong ETH, characterized by Gaussian statistics in off-diagonal matrix elements and $D^{-1/2}$ scaling of diagonal fluctuations.

**Method.** The study employs classical analysis (phase-space portraits, KSE, and ergodicity testing) alongside quantum analysis (ETH ansatz testing, spectral form factor, and higher-order spacing ratio statistics).

**Model / system.** A many-body system derived from the Double Kicked Top (DKT) model, consisting of $N=2j$ spin-1/2 particles with all-to-all interactions, characterized by kick strengths $k_r$ and $k_{	heta}$.

**Key observables.** Diagonal and off-diagonal matrix elements of the observable $J_z^2 / [j(j+1)]$, Kolmogorov-Sinai entropy (KSE), von Neumann entropy, and spacing ratio statistics $r(	ilde{k})$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Kick strengths $k_r$ (radial) and $k_{	heta}$ (angular); regimes include non-chaotic/non-ergodic, non-chaotic/mean-ergodic, and chaotic.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the validity of the Floquet operator formalism and the applicability of von Neumann's mean-ergodic theorem to the classical limit.

**Figures summary.** Figures include phase-space portraits, plots of expectation values and fluctuations, distributions of matrix elements, and spectral diagnostics like the Spectral Form Factor and spacing ratio distributions.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of ETH in non-chaotic systems, describes the DKT model, presents classical ergodicity and chaos analysis, moves to quantum ETH verification via matrix element statistics, and concludes with spectral and correlation-based proofs of integrability/chaos.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We report an example of a many-body system, derived from the double kicked top (DKT), with non-chaotic yet mean-ergodic dynamics that displays \textit{strong} eigenstate thermalization hypothesis (ETH) in the quantum regime. The analysis addresses a key open question: whether \textit{strong} ETH is a quantum analog of ergodicity (or mean-ergodicity). Despite non-chaotic dynamics, the fluctuations of the diagonal matrix elements of an observable scale as $D^{-1/2}$, where $D$ denotes the Hilbert space dimension. Furthermore, the off-diagonal matrix elements show Gaussian statistics together with a smooth function $f_O(\bar{E}, ω)$ that becomes nearly uniform in the large-$k_θ$ domain. Our findings show that even mean-ergodic and non-chaotic systems can exhibit \textit{strong} ETH.

</details>

### [A Variational Dissipative Framework for Quantum Algorithms](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25841v1)

**Authors:** Yuan Yao, Ruipeng Xing, Yongjian Gu, Yiming Huang, Xiao Yuan  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.25841v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `methods for driven-dissipative` **4/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5** · `quantum measurements` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">

<img src="2605.25841_figures/2605.25841_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">

</figure>

Panels (a) and (b) cor- respond to different main-system sizes. </figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.25841_figures/2605.25841_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Figure 3: Fidelity recovery performance for different numbers of ancillas. Introducing dissipative ancillas sig- nificantly improves the fidelity beyond the initial input value, and increasing the number of ancillas generally leads to both faster recovery and a higher final fidelity.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.25841_figures/2605.25841_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">

(a) Compari- son under amplitude-damping, bit-flip, and depolarizing noise in the fully noisy setting. (b) Comparison for three representative Hamiltonians: an XX-type model in a transverse field, a transverse-field Ising model, and an XXZ-type model. In both cases, the dissipative VQE converges faster and reaches lower final energies than the ancilla-free baseline.</figcaption>
</figure>
