(a) Recovery of a dressed cluster target state un- der depolarizing, bit-flip, and amplitude-damping noise. (b) Recovery performance for three representative target states: the W state |ψW ⟩, the uniform superposition state |ψ+⟩, and the dressed cluster state |ψDC⟩. Unless otherwise specified, the simulations use n = 3 system qubits, m = 3 ancillas, learning rate lr = 0.8, dissipa- tive rounds T = 3, and noise strength p = 0.1.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a new variational framework that incorporates engineered dissipation into quantum algorithms using ancilla-assisted channels. It demonstrates that these dissipative modules can be trained to both find ground states more efficiently and recover noisy states, potentially offering an exponential speedup in optimization complexity.

**Why it may be interesting.** This is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems as it treats dissipation not as a nuisance but as a programmable resource for state engineering and error mitigation, providing a bridge between reservoir engineering and quantum algorithm design.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Standard variational quantum algorithms are limited to unitary operations, which restricts the ansatz to pure states and makes them susceptible to noise-induced barren plateaus. The research seeks to expand the variational search space by incorporating controllable, nonunitary dissipative processes.

**Main result.** The authors propose a unified dissipative framework that uses ancilla-assisted engineered dissipation as a trainable resource to both improve ground-state energy convergence and perform state recovery. They theoretically demonstrate a conditional exponential advantage in iteration complexity over standard unitary VQAs under certain landscape conditions.

**Method.** The framework integrates trainable dissipative modules, realized via system-ancilla unitaries followed by ancilla reset and trace-out, into parameterized quantum circuits. The authors use the GKLS master equation, Stinespring dilation, and the Polyak-Lojasiewicz (PL) inequality to analyze convergence and complexity.

**Model / system.** The model consists of a system of n qubits coupled to an m-qubit ancilla register. The dynamics are described by a combination of system-only unitary layers and ancilla-assisted dissipative blocks, tested on spin Hamiltonians including XX-type, transverse-field Ising, and XXZ models.

**Key observables.** Ground-state energy expectation value, state fidelity, convergence speed (energy descent), and iteration complexity.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Number of system qubits (n), number of ancillas (m), number of dissipative rounds (T), noise strength (p), and learning rate (lr).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Relies on the Born-Markov approximation, the assumption that the dissipative objective satisfies the PL condition, and that ancillas are reset to the zero state before each dissipative block.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the general architecture; Figure 2 and 3 demonstrate improved energy convergence and fidelity recovery with increasing ancilla counts; Figure 4 and 5 show robustness across different noise channels and target states; Figure 7 illustrates ancilla saturation and a robustness plateau.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the limitation of unitary ansätze, proposes the dissipative framework, details the implementation for ground-state search and state recovery, provides numerical evidence of performance gains and robustness, and concludes with a mathematical complexity analysis comparing dissipative and unitary VQAs.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Dissipation engineering has attracted growing interest as an approach to controlling open quantum systems through engineered system-environment interactions. Standard variational quantum circuits are usually built from unitary operations and therefore explore only a restricted family of states. To go beyond this limitation, we introduce a variational dissipative framework in which ancilla-assisted engineered dissipation is incorporated into parameterized quantum algorithms. In this framework, system-only variational layers are combined with trainable dissipative modules, so that the circuit can prepare a broader class of mixed states through ancilla-assisted nonunitary transformations. Within this framework, the same ancilla-assisted dissipative block is used in two representative settings with different objectives. For ground-state search, it is integrated into a dissipative variational quantum eigensolver to improve the convergence toward low-energy states. For state recovery, it is trained as a recovery channel to suppress preparation noise and enhance fidelity with the target state. In both cases, the block is realized through parameterized system-ancilla couplings followed by ancilla reset and trace-out. Our results show that engineered dissipation can be incorporated into variational quantum circuits as a reusable trainable primitive rather than treated only as a source of noise. In this sense, the proposed framework identifies ancilla-assisted dissipative channels as a concrete variational resource that can support both optimization and recovery tasks within a unified design.

</details>

### [Boundary $0/π$ logical subspace and bulk dynamical probes in flux-controlled anomalous Floquet quantum walks](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25792v1)

**Authors:** WeiCheng Ning, YanSheng Liu, XiaoXue Zhang, XiZheng Zhang

Panels (a) and (b) show W1 and W2 evaluated on a high-resolution grid in the (M, ϕ) plane. The representative parameter sets used later in Figs. 2, 3, and 4 are written directly on the phase map. Circular markers denote the open boundary points Pj, square markers denote the frame-resolved bulk points Qj, and diamond markers denote the critical benchmarks C0 and Cπ. Every later numerical panel can therefore be traced back to a definite point in the bulk diagram.</figcaption>

**Summary.** This paper shows how a flux-controlled quantum walk can host a protected 0/pi logical subspace at its boundaries while allowing the bulk topology to be read out via dynamical probes. It identifies a unique doubled-period response in the coexistence sector and distinguishes between different topological gaps using local stroboscopic signatures. This suggests a robust route for implementing quantum-walk-based information primitives in driven microstructured lattices.

**Why it may be interesting.** It presents a unified framework for creating symmetry-protected quantum information primitives (edge qubits) and provides specific dynamical signatures for detecting anomalous Floquet topology in driven lattice systems.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating whether a single microscopic family of driven lattices can simultaneously support a boundary logical subspace (0/pi edge qubit) and detectable bulk topological diagnostics via a flux-controlled protocol.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that a flux-controlled anomalous Floquet quantum walk provides a symmetry-protected boundary logical subspace with a doubled-period (2T) response and that the bulk topology can be dynamically resolved through frame-resolved mean chiral displacements.

**Method.** The study uses momentum-space Bloch spinors, Taylor expansions around critical gap-closing points, and numerical simulations of both periodic and open boundary condition chains.

**Model / system.** A one-dimensional, chiral, flux-controlled anomalous Floquet quantum walk realized in a driven bipartite lattice with a coin-dependent drift step and a momentum-dependent mixing step.

**Key observables.** Boundary local observables (site-one and boundary window projectors), frame-resolved mean chiral displacement, and local stroboscopic return probability.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Mass parameter (M), flux phase (phi), hopping amplitudes (t1, t2), and driving period (T).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes a clean system (pre-reflection window) and uses small-parameter expansions near gap-closing points.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the bulk topological phase diagram in the (M, phi) plane; Figure 2 displays the quasienergy spectrum at the edge; Figure 4 compares the return probability and stroboscopic response at the 0-gap and pi-gap benchmarks.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the microscopic model, defines the topological classification and phase diagram, analyzes the boundary logical subspace dynamics, and concludes with bulk dynamical probes and gap-specific stroboscopic signatures.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We formulate a one-dimensional flux-controlled anomalous Floquet quantum walk and show that it admits a direct microscopic realization in a driven bipartite lattice. The walk consists of a coin-dependent drift step and a momentum-dependent coin mixing step, so the same evolution operator governs quasienergy bands, boundary modes, and bulk dynamics in real space. Because the walk is chiral, the quasienergy gaps at $0$ and $π/T$ carry independent topological information, which organizes trivial, $0$-only, $π$-only, and coexistence sectors in the $(M,φ)$ plane. In the coexistence sector, a $0$ mode and a $π$ mode reside on the same edge and span a natural boundary logical subspace. One Floquet period acts there as a relative phase operation and produces a clear $2T$ response in local boundary observables. In the bulk, the same anomalous Floquet structure is probed dynamically in two complementary ways. Frame-resolved mean chiral displacements approach the two winding numbers in the clean pre-reflection window of the symmetric time frames, while selected benchmark cuts at a representative $0$ gap closing and a representative $π$ gap closing exhibit distinct local stroboscopic responses, with the $π$ gap benchmark showing a much stronger odd-even alternation. The boundary logical subspace and the bulk dynamical probes are therefore organized within one flux-controlled anomalous Floquet quantum walk, suggesting a symmetry-protected route to quantum-walk information primitives in driven microstructured lattices.

</details>

### [Dynamical susceptibility and quantum Fisher information in the Su-Schrieffer-Heeger model with Hatsugai-Kohmoto interactions](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25231v1)

**Authors:** Sepide Mohamadi, Jahanfar Abouie  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.25231v1>

**Summary.** This paper explores the dynamical properties of the SSH model when augmented with long-range Hatsugai-Kohmoto interactions. It demonstrates that while the Quantum Fisher Information can effectively distinguish between different particle filling sectors, it fails to detect topological phase transitions in uniform-density regimes. The work highlights how the interplay between dimerization and specific interaction types can create unique spectral features not found in standard Hubbard models.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides an analytically tractable platform to study how long-range, momentum-diagonal interactions compete with topological band structures and how entanglement-related quantities like QFI can serve as probes for many-body phases.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates the dynamical response properties and the Quantum Fisher Information (QFI) in the Su-Schrieffer-Heeger (SSH) model with Hatsugai-Kohmoto (HK) interactions, specifically exploring the competition between topology and strong correlations.

**Main result.** The SSH-HK model shows qualitatively distinct behavior from Hubbard models, including a finite response at zero wave vector and a piecewise QFI structure that distinguishes integer from fractional filling sectors, though the QFI remains insensitive to topological transitions in uniform-density regimes.

**Method.** The authors leverage the exact solvability of the HK term to derive closed-form expressions for dynamical susceptibility and use the Random Phase Approximation (RPA) for comparison with Hubbard-type models.

**Model / system.** The primary model is the SSH-HK model, which combines the 1D dimerized SSH lattice (with alternating hopping amplitudes v and w) with momentum-diagonal, long-range Hatsugai-Kohmoto interactions.

**Key observables.** Dynamical spin and charge susceptibilities (Im chi), Quantum Fisher Information (QFI), spectral weight, and topological invariants (Zak phase).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Hopping amplitudes (v, w), interaction strength (U), wave vector (q), frequency (omega), and particle filling (n).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The comparison models (Hubbard and SSH-Hubbard) are treated within the Random Phase Approximation (RPA); the HK model is treated via its exact solvability.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the evolution of dynamical susceptibility across different frequencies and wave vectors, the piecewise behavior of the QFI across different filling sectors, and the phase boundaries between trivial and topological phases.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the SSH-HK model, provides an analytical benchmark by comparing it to Hubbard-type models using RPA, derives closed-form expressions for the SSH-HK susceptibility, analyzes the QFI as a probe for filling sectors, and discusses the limitations of these probes in detecting topological transitions.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate the dynamical spin and charge susceptibilities and the associated quantum Fisher information in a class of interacting lattice models, with a primary focus on the Su-Schrieffer-Heeger model in the presence of Hatsugai-Kohmoto interactions. To provide a rigorous analytical benchmark, we contrast the response properties of the SSH-HK system with those of the single-band Hubbard and SSH-Hubbard models, treated within the random-phase approximation. While standard Hubbard-type interactions typically suppress excitation strength, we demonstrate that the SSH-HK model displays qualitatively distinct physical behavior arising from the interplay between SSH dimerization and the momentum-diagonal nature of the HK interaction. Leveraging the exact solvability of the HK term, we derive closed-form expressions for the dynamical susceptibility, revealing unique filling-controlled characteristics such as a finite response at zero wave vector and a pronounced restructuring of spectral weight across integer and fractional filling sectors. We show that the quantum Fisher information, defined as the frequency integral of the imaginary part of the susceptibility, serves as an efficient probe of these filling sectors, exhibiting distinct piecewise behavior that distinguishes integer from fractional fillings. Notably, our results indicate that the quantum Fisher information remains insensitive to topological transitions within uniform-density regimes, highlighting the limitations of standard dynamical response functions in characterizing band topology. These findings establish the SSH-HK model as a powerful analytical platform for exploring the competition between topology and strong correlations, demonstrating how dynamical susceptibilities and the quantum Fisher information provide complementary, experimentally accessible probes of many-body physics.

</details>

### [Unified Flux Control Architecture for Fluxonium Qubits](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25948v1)

**Authors:** Xianchuang Pan, Jiahui Wang, Tao Zhou, Yanbo Guo, Fei Wang, Ze Zhan, Liang Xiang, Zishuo Li, Lu Ma, Xizheng Ma, Huijuan Zhan, Tao Zhang, Kannan Lu, Xing Zhu, Guicheng Gong, Chunqing Deng, Tenghui Wang

(b) Waveform synthesis process. Left: stored primitives, including baseband flux edges and a resonant cosine envelope. Middle: assembled and modulated waveforms prior to filtering. **Summary.** This paper presents a way to control fluxonium qubits using only one control line for both rotation and tuning, significantly reducing the amount of hardware needed per qubit. By using digital filters to fix the signal distortion caused by the necessary noise-suppression filters, the authors achieve extremely high gate fidelities and efficient qubit reset. This architecture provides a scalable path forward for building larger superconducting quantum processors.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is relevant to open quantum systems and quantum optics researchers as it demonstrates a sophisticated method for managing the trade-off between environmental noise suppression (decoherence control) and strong-coupling-driven control (system manipulation) in a single-port architecture.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The scaling of superconducting quantum processors is limited by the hardware overhead of separate control channels for XY and Z operations, which also creates a conflict between needing high attenuation for coherence and low attenuation for rapid qubit reset.

**Main result.** The authors experimentally realize a unified flux control architecture for fluxonium qubits that achieves single-qubit gate fidelities exceeding 99.99% and active reset fidelity of approximately 98% using a single control channel.

**Method.** The approach uses frequency-selective cryogenic filtering combined with compensated waveform synthesis via FPGA-native instruction-level FIR/IIR filtering to correct for pulse distortion caused by the low-pass control line.

**Model / system.** The system consists of fluxonium qubits (superconducting circuits) coupled to a readout resonator, controlled via a single inductive flux channel that provides both transverse (AC) and longitudinal (DC) control.

**Key observables.** Coherence times (T1, T2, T2-echo), single-qubit gate fidelities, active reset fidelity, Rabi frequency, and pulse distortion/settling dynamics.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Coherence times > 100 us, gate fidelities > 99.99%, 20-ns gate durations, 92 MHz low-pass filter cutoff, and fluxonium parameters Ej/h = 4.5 GHz, Ec/h = 1.1 GHz.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The noise is modeled as frequency-independent white noise from the AWG and Johnson-Nyquist thermal noise; the microwave tail is assumed to be short enough that no additional correction is required.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the circuit schematic, energy spectrum, and control protocol; Fig 6 and 7 illustrate the characterization of quadrature distortions and the performance of the FIR-based waveform compensation.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the scaling bottleneck of control hardware, describes the unified fluxonium architecture, details the hardware-software compensation methods (filtering and synthesis), presents experimental validation of coherence and gate fidelity, and concludes with the scalability of the FPGA-based approach.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Control architectures that reduce hardware overhead while maintaining high-fidelity operations are essential for the continued scaling of superconducting quantum processors. Here we experimentally realize a unified control architecture for fluxonium qubits, in which both transverse ($XY$) and longitudinal ($Z$) operations are implemented through a single flux-control channel driven by a single arbitrary waveform generator channel. This architecture imposes competing requirements on the shared control channel, which must simultaneously support low-frequency flux transmission for reset operations while strongly attenuating broadband noise near the qubit transition frequency. We address this challenge through frequency-selective cryogenic filtering together with compensated waveform synthesis that corrects the pulse distortion introduced by the filtered control line. Experimentally, this approach preserves coherence times above 100 $μ$s while enabling active reset with approximately 98% fidelity and 20-ns single-qubit gates with fidelities exceeding 99.99%. We further demonstrate FPGA-native instruction-level waveform synthesis based on reusable pulse primitives for unified flux control. These results establish unified flux control as a scalable architecture for fluxonium qubits that reduces control hardware overhead while preserving high-fidelity operation.

</details>

### [Native topological readout on qubit hardware: a Fibonacci-chain benchmark of measurement-compilation trade-offs](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25913v1)

**Authors:** Babatunde Moses Ayeni  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.25913v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `QC/QI experiment` **4/5** · `quantum measurements` **3/5**

Marker- only points show the empirical MSE at the sampled budgets, using circles for FR and squares for PSQWC. The overlaid solid FR and dashed PS-QWC lines are least-squares fits of the form αm + βm/N to the empirical mean-squared-error traces. The figure makes the fitted empirical-MSE scaling visible directly in the budget traces.</figcaption>
</figure>

The point markers show the empirical MSE values for FR and PSQWC, while the solid and dashed lines show the fitted laws αm + βm/N, and dashed vertical lines mark fit positive-budget crossings that fall inside the sampled budget window. Panel annotations report the fitted crossing budget when it exists and indicate whether it lies below, within, or above the sampled range.</figcaption>

Point markers show the empirical MSE values for FR and PSQWC, while solid and dashed lines show the fitted laws αm + βm/N, and dashed vertical lines mark fit positive-budget crossings that fall inside the sampled budget window. The VQE panels make clear that most fitted crossings for this workload already sit below the sampled budget range, consistent with the small fitted Nc values reported in the main text.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper investigates whether measuring in the 'native' basis of a topological model is more efficient than standard Pauli-basis reconstruction on noisy quantum hardware. It finds that while native readout reduces sampling variance, the extra circuit depth required can introduce enough noise to make standard Pauli measurements more accurate for shallow variational circuits.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Determining the optimal measurement strategy for topological Hamiltonians on NISQ hardware by evaluating the trade-off between the sampling advantage of native (fusion) readout and the increased hardware error/bias introduced by additional compilation depth.

**Main result.** While native fusion readout (FR) provides a universal advantage in sampling variance, its advantage in Mean Squared Error (MSE) is diminished or reversed in shallow circuits (like VQE) due to hardware-induced noise from the additional basis-change gates.

**Method.** The study compares Fusion Readout (FR) against a Grouped Pauli (PSQWC) baseline using both noiseless simulations and experiments on the IBM 'ibm_pittsburgh' superconducting processor, employing MSE scaling analysis to identify crossover shot budgets.

**Model / system.** A one-dimensional Fibonacci anyon chain model using fusion-path encoding, featuring Hamiltonians with both braiding and fusion interactions ($H_{F3}$ and $H_{BF4}$), implemented on a heavy-hex superconducting qubit architecture.

**Key observables.** Fusion-channel projectors, local interaction terms, energy estimator Mean Squared Error (MSE), and covariance-aware sampling variance.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Qubit number (n=5 to 11), shot budget (N=2000 to 16000), Trotter step count (s=1, 3, 6), and the crossover shot budget (Nc).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes the Fibonacci anyons are modeled via fusion-space encoding rather than physical anyonic excitations, and the Pauli baseline is chosen for transparency rather than being the most aggressive possible estimator.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates the basis change for native measurement; Figure 2 shows heatmaps of MSE and sampling variance ratios; Figure 3 compares VQE hardware benchmarks; Figure 4 displays MSE scaling fits; Figure 5 shows digital hardware MSE scaling; Figure 7 and 8 show correlations between measurement overhead and error ratios.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the motivation for native readout, defines the Fibonacci chain model and measurement strategies, presents results from noiseless simulations, details hardware benchmarks for digital Floquet and VQE circuits, and concludes with a scaling analysis of the crossover point between methods.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Recent demonstrations of non-Abelian braiding of graph vertices on noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) superconducting processor, and the experimental realization of topological order in general on various quantum hardware platforms necessitate an important question: when does a native (topological) fusion readout genuinely help for topological anyonic Hamiltonians implemented on NISQ hardware? We use the Fibonacci anyons chain as a concrete model for understanding the trade-off between measurement cost and compilation cost in that setting. The comparison is made against a simple grouped-Pauli baseline, and is scored by a covariance-aware mean-squared-error (MSE) of the full energy estimator. We based our benchmark on two different important classes of quantum circuits, namely Floquet time-evolved and variational quantum eigensolver quantum circuits, with the underlying Hamiltonian consisting of both braiding and fusion interaction. Our analysis found that there is not a uniform best method across both problems: the fusion readout method performed better on Floquet-type circuits on both the MSE and covariance-aware sampling variance, while the grouped Pauli method performed better on VQE on the MSE but worse on sampling variance. We derive scaling laws, and compute shot-budget crossover points, where one method is operationally favored above the other. The relevance of this work extends beyond Fibonacci chains to two-dimensional topological models compiled on superconducting and other qubit-native platforms, and can be used as a guide in answering the question of when one should measure in the native operator basis of the target physics, or when it is better to fall back on Pauli-basis reconstruction.

</details>

**Authors:** Zhuoran Bao, Daniel F. V. James  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.24108v1>

</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.24108_figures/2605.24108_page5.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Low-resolution page preview, page 5</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.24108_figures/2605.24108_page6.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Low-resolution page preview, page 6</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** The paper proposes a practical measurement scheme using Bell state analysis to estimate rotation angles around an unknown axis. It proves that this method can achieve the fundamental precision limits (QCRB) for N=4 and N=6 anti-coherent states in the small-angle regime. This overcomes the complexity of full state tomography for these highly entangled states.

**Why it may be interesting.** This is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum information as it provides a practical, efficient measurement protocol for highly entangled states that are otherwise difficult to utilize for precision sensing.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the difficulty of extracting rotation angles in quantum sensing when using second-order anti-coherent states, which are known to be optimal but are complex to measure via standard tomography.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that a measurement scheme based on pair-wise Bell state analysis with an additional path degree of freedom can saturate the Quantum Cramer-Rao Bound for small rotation angles.

**Method.** The study uses a mathematical framework of angular momentum operators and Fisher information derivation, applying a small-angle approximation to evaluate the performance of Bell state projections.

**Model / system.** The system consists of N-photon polarization states (N-qubit/qupon states) in the symmetric subspace, specifically focusing on N=4 and N=6 second-order anti-coherent (unpolarized) states undergoing rotation around an unknown axis.

**Key observables.** Rotation angle theta_1, probabilities of Bell state projections, and the Fisher information matrix.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Small rotation angle regime (theta_1 -> 0), N=4 and N=6 photon numbers, and the rotation axis vector u.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The validity of the optimal measurement is demonstrated specifically in the limit of small rotation angles, neglecting higher-order terms of O(theta_1^3).

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining the problem of parameter extraction in rotation sensing, introduces the physical model of N-photon states and the rotation operator, develops the Fisher information derivation and optimization strategy, proposes a Bell state analysis measurement scheme, and concludes by demonstrating the saturation of the QCRB for N=4 and N=6 cases.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The second-order anti-coherent state of light is known to saturate the Cramer-Rao Bound (QCRB) for rotation sensing around an arbitrary axis. However, due to the complexity of the state and the inefficiency of state tomography, parameter extraction remains an open problem. In this manuscript, we approach the problem of parameter extraction using pair-wise Bell state analysis with an additional path degree of freedom. Due to the transformation property of rotation, only the symmetric Bell states will show up in projection in the final state. We exploit this advantage to develop a scheme for extracting the rotation angle for N=4 and N=6 second-order anti-coherent states.

</details>

### [Evaluating System-Level Fidelity with Peaked Random Circuits](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25983v1)

**Authors:** Martin Brieger, Florian Krötz, Minh Chung, Dieter Kranzlmüller  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.25983v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `QC/QI experiment` **4/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **1/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **1/5**

White cells indicate skipped runs resulting from previously run, lower-depth circuits yielding non- identifications. The black line separates executed circuits from skipped ones. </figure>

</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a new benchmarking framework using Peaked Random Circuits to evaluate the fidelity of NISQ quantum hardware. By comparing superconducting and trapped-ion processors, the authors demonstrate that PRCs can sensitively detect hardware-specific noise pathologies and provide a more robust metric for cross-platform comparison than Quantum Volume.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a new way to quantify how environmental noise and decoherence specifically wash out constructive interference patterns, which is highly relevant to the study of open quantum systems and the stability of many-body states.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The lack of architecture-agnostic performance metrics to compare diverse NISQ hardware platforms and the limitations of existing benchmarks like Quantum Volume in capturing system-level fidelity.

**Main result.** Peaked Random Circuits (PRCs) provide a high-precision, scalable benchmarking metric that is more sensitive to interference effects and noise than Quantum Volume, successfully distinguishing between superconducting and trapped-ion performance.

**Method.** The authors repurpose PRCs by optimizing a 'peaking half' of a mirror circuit to maximize a target bitstring probability and then apply this as a benchmark across varying qubit counts and depths.

**Model / system.** The study uses two commercial NISQ platforms: IQM's QExa20 (superconducting, square-lattice) and AQT's AQT20 (trapped-ion, all-to-all connectivity).

**Key observables.** Peak identification (binary success/fail), Peak Dominance (Rp), Relative Peakedness (Cexp), and Fidelity Error (F).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Qubit counts (n) from 2 to 20, circuit depths (d) from 2 to 50, and the number of shots (up to 1,000,000).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The target bitstring is known, the input is the all-zero state, and for odd-depth circuits, the random half is shortened to prevent diminishing peak probability.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the PRC mirror architecture; Fig 2 illustrates peak identification vs. non-identification via histograms; Fig 3 shows peak probability scaling; Fig 4 displays a 2D grid of successful/failed runs; Fig 5 shows a heatmap of performance differences between the two platforms.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the need for new metrics, describes the PRC architecture and optimization method, presents the benchmarking protocol on two hardware platforms, analyzes the results comparing superconducting and trapped-ion systems, and discusses limitations and future directions.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum computing is transitioning from experimental prototypes to commercially available turnkey systems, making architecture-agnostic performance metrics essential for cross-platform comparison. Peaked Random Circuits (PRCs) have recently been proposed as a viable path to demonstrate quantum advantage on NISQ devices: a quantum processor can reliably detect a single, peaked output state amid background noise, yet the circuits' characteristics render classical simulation infeasible. In this paper, we repurpose PRCs as a system-level fidelity benchmark. By successively running a matrix of PRCs with varying qubit counts and circuit depths, we quantify a system's ability to identify the deterministic peak despite cumulative noise, gate errors, and connectivity constraints. We apply the benchmark on IQM's superconducting and AQT's trapped-ion architectures. Our results show that PRCs provide a high-precision metric comparable to Quantum Volume while exhibiting greater sensitivity to interference effects. Consequently, PRCs enable a robust framework for assessing the computational reliability of NISQ hardware across platforms.

</details>

### [Krylov Complexity for Plane Wave Matrix Model](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26055v1)

**Authors:** Dibakar Roychowdhury  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.26055v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `entanglement & information structure` **4/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.26055_figures/2605.26055_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1. We plot early time growth in complexity for different values of the Coulmb charge Q.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.26055_figures/2605.26055_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 2. Plot the LO coefficient ζ with the Coulomb charge Q.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper explores how mass deformation influences the growth of complexity in the BMN matrix model. It demonstrates that both state and operator complexities share a universal quadratic time-dependence at early stages and that Lanczos coefficients scale linearly with the mass parameter.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the growth of Krylov state complexity (KSC) and Krylov operator complexity (KOC) in the BMN Plane Wave Matrix Model, specifically focusing on how mass deformation affects complexity growth.

**Main result.** The study finds that Lanczos coefficients scale linearly with the mass deformation parameter (mu) and that early-time complexity growth exhibits a universal quadratic correction proportional to (mu^2 * t^2).

**Method.** The authors use Hamiltonian analysis and the Lanczos algorithm on reduced matrix models (N=3, N=4) in the large mass deformation limit.

**Model / system.** The BMN Plane Wave Matrix Model, which is a massive deformation of the BFSS matrix model describing membranes on an 11d pp-wave background, including reduced models like the Fuzzy Sphere and Coulomb potential models.

**Key observables.** Krylov state complexity (KSC), Krylov operator complexity (KOC), and Lanczos coefficients (a_n and b_n).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Mass deformation parameter (mu), Coulomb charge (Q), and the large mass deformation limit (mu -> infinity).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis is restricted to the bosonic sector, assumes the large mass deformation limit, and utilizes the Weyl gauge (A=0).

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the early-time growth of complexity for different models; Figure 2 illustrates the dependence of the complexity pre-factor (zeta) on the Coulomb charge (Q), showing a non-monotonic behavior.

**Paper structure.** The paper is structured into two main parts: the first analyzes Krylov state complexity using N=3 and N=4 reductions, and the second investigates Krylov operator growth, followed by a comparative analysis of scaling laws and early-time dynamics.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study Krylov complexity in BMN Plane Wave Matrix Model at large mass deformation. We consider various consistent reductions of the matrix model that allow us to perform a Hamiltonian analysis which leads to different notions of the Krylov complexity. In the first part of the paper, we study the Krylov state complexity considering systematic reduction of $N=3$ and $N=4$ representations of the matrix model, which reveals a universal characteristic scaling for the Lanczos coefficients and fix them completely in terms of the mass deformation parameter. In the second part of the paper, we study the Krylov operator growth in the matrix model and compute the corresponding Lanczos coefficients. In both cases, we observe a \emph{linear} scaling of Lanczos coefficients with the mass parameter. The early time growth in Krylov complexity receives quadratic correction due to the presence of the massive deformation in the matrix model. Our analysis reveals that such massive corrections appear at same order in time for both the notion of the Krylov complexity.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper studies how anisotropic, sign-changing dipolar interactions in a molecular lattice simulator drive the formation of a stripe-family ordered phase. It shows that the interaction geometry selects specific axial stripes and that the transition to this phase is a first-order process. The findings are linked to realistic experimental scales for NaCs polar molecules.

**Why it may be interesting.** It demonstrates how engineering the angular dependence of long-range interactions can be used as a primary tool to program complex many-body phases and density-wave patterns in programmable quantum simulators.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating how programmable, non-axisymmetric, and sign-changing dipolar interaction geometries select specific density-ordering patterns (stripe-family order) in a molecular lattice quantum simulator.

**Main result.** The interaction geometry selects an axial (q,0) stripe-family where the wave vector evolves with filling, and the transition from superfluid to stripe-solid is identified as a first-order switching rather than a stable supersolid phase.

**Method.** Worm-algorithm path-integral quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) simulations, supported by a hard-core spin mapping and a Gutzwiller soft-mode diagnostic.

**Model / system.** A 2D square optical lattice of microwave-dressed polar molecules (specifically NaCs) modeled as a minimal hard-core Bose-Hubbard model with a non-axisymmetric, sign-changing dipolar tail.

**Key observables.** Directional superfluid stiffness anisotropy, structure factor S(q), compressibility, and measurement-resolved structure-factor histograms.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Ratio of hopping to interaction strength (t/V), chemical potential (mu), and lattice filling (n).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The use of a strict hard-core constraint (infinite onsite repulsion) and the deep-lattice/point-particle approximation.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the lattice interaction geometry; Figure 2 presents the ground-state phase diagram; Figure 7 shows Gutzwiller diagnostics for stripe period; Figure 8 shows real-space density maps.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the programmable interaction geometry, presents the numerical phase diagram and phase transition analysis, provides a Gutzwiller-based mechanism for stripe selection, and concludes with an experimental feasibility study for NaCs molecules.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Microwave-dressed polar molecules offer a route to lattice quantum simulators in which the angular form of long-range dipolar interactions, not only their overall strength, can be engineered. We study this setting in a minimal hard-core Bose lattice model on a square optical lattice, with particles interacting through a sign-changing non-axisymmetric dipolar tail \mathcal V(\mathbf r)\propto (x^2-y^2)/(x^2+y^2)^{5/2} that is repulsive along one lattice axis and attractive along the other. Using worm-algorithm path-integral quantum Monte Carlo simulations, supported by a hard-core spin mapping and a Gutzwiller soft-mode diagnostic, we find two regimes controlled by t/V: at larger t/V the system remains superfluid but develops a pronounced directional stiffness anisotropy, while at smaller t/V it forms a stripe solid selected in the (q,0) axial family, corresponding to real-space stripes parallel to y. The leading ordering wave vector remains in this axial family but reorganizes with filling, showing that the robust ordered object is a family of stripe states rather than one fixed commensurate Bragg peak. Near the closure of the stripe lobe, averaged observables can mimic a narrow supersolid signal; measurement-resolved stripe structure-factor histograms instead reveal first-order switching between superfluid and stripe-solid sectors. NaCs lattice estimates place the relevant V/t window within reach of modest effective dressed dipole moments, linking the predicted stripe-family order and its experimental diagnostics to accessible molecular quantum-simulation scales.

</details>

### [Pseudorandom Dynamics in the SYK Model and Cryptographic Censorship in JT Gravity](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25178v1)

**Authors:** Pouya Golmohammadi  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.25178v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `entanglement & information structure` **4/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.25178_figures/2605.25178_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1: Numerical verification of the frame potential analysis for SYK4 with N = 8, 10, 12 (L = 16, 32, 64), averaged over 100–300 disorder realizations. (a) The first frame potential F(1)(T) computed directly from cross-realization averages of |tr(U † 1U2)|2 (solid), compared with the analytic formula (1.1) evaluated from the disorder-averaged SFF (dashed). (b) The deviation F(1)(T) −1 as a function of T/TH, showing the approach to the τ 2 = (T/TH)2 scaling predicted by (3.9) (dashed gray line). (c) The normalized spectral form factor SFF(T)/L, exhibiting the universal slope–dip– ramp–plateau structure. Solid lines show the connected SFF; dotted lines show the disconnected part.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that the SYK model functions as a pseudorandom unitary that hides information from efficient observers, effectively realizing 'Cryptographic Censorship' in JT gravity. It shows that the existence of an event horizon is necessary to prevent an adversary from reconstructing the black hole interior. The work connects the growth of black hole complexity to the limits of quantum information reconstruction.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a deep link between computational complexity (pseudorandomness and hardness) and fundamental gravitational phenomena (event horizons and black hole interiors), which is highly relevant to researchers studying the intersection of quantum information and many-body dynamics.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Determining whether the SYK model acts as a gravitationally pseudorandom unitary (GPRU) and whether this pseudorandomness enforces the existence of event horizons in its JT gravity dual (Cryptographic Censorship).

**Main result.** The paper proves the SYK disorder ensemble is an approximate unitary k-design and, under the planted-SYK hardness conjecture, a GPRU, implying that typical states must have event horizons to prevent efficient reconstruction of the black hole interior.

**Method.** The author employs Weingarten calculus, random matrix theory, and complexity-theoretic frameworks like shadow tomography and the low-degree polynomial framework to analyze the SYK ensemble.

**Model / system.** The SYK model, consisting of N Majorana fermions with all-to-all q-body random interactions, and its holographic dual, JT gravity (2D dilaton gravity).

**Key observables.** Spectral Form Factor (SFF), k-th frame potential, regularized geodesic length of the maximal interior slice, and prediction fidelity.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Number of fermions (N), scrambling time (T_scr), Heisenberg time (T_H), and the security parameter (kappa).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The 'planted-SYK hardness conjecture' (computational indistinguishability from Haar) and random matrix universality.

**Figures summary.** Not specified in the provided notes.

**Paper structure.** The paper establishes the statistical properties of the SYK ensemble as a k-design, introduces the planted-SYK hardness conjecture to define GPRU status, proves the Cryptographic Censorship theorem via reconstruction limits, and links the results to the complexity equals volume conjecture.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We argue that the SYK model provides a conditional realization of Cryptographic Censorship in JT gravity. By using the Weingarten calculus and random matrix universality, we prove that the SYK disorder ensemble is an approximate unitary $k$-design for all $k=\poly(N)$, with deviation controlled by the spectral form factor. We then formulate the planted-SYK hardness conjecture and provide evidence from spectral universality and the low-degree polynomial framework. Under this conjecture, the approximate design becomes a gravitationally pseudorandom unitary. Together with the efficient causal wedge reconstruction in JT gravity, this leads to the conclusion that typical states in the SYK microcanonical window must have event horizons in their bulk duals, with the horizonless fraction doubly exponentially small. We further identify the regularized geodesic length of the maximal interior slice as the explicit distinguishing operator. Its prediction gap grows linearly with time due to the stretching of the black hole interior, linking Cryptographic Censorship to the complexity equals volume conjecture.

</details>

### [Quantum non-demolition measurements as a practical primitive for fault-tolerant computation against biased noise](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24262v1)

**Authors:** Christophe Vuillot, Diego Ruiz, Jérémie Guillaud, Mazyar Mirrahimi  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.24262v1>

Moreover, the assignment error of the Z⊗3 measure- ment has to be as small as the bit-flip error probability for the protocol to preserve the bias. (b) Circuit to implement an X⊗2 parity check using a single QND Z⊗4 measurement. The measurement outcome is given by m = m1 ⊕m2. (c) Circuit to implement a ZXXZ measurement using a single QND Z⊗4 measurement. The measurement outcome is given by m = m1 ⊕m2. It can be obtained by conjugating the XX measurement in...</figcaption>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 3. Four nuclear spins (in red) are coupled to an electron spin (in blue) through hyperfine interaction and the electron spin undergoes an optical or electrical readout. Such a setup could be used to perform high-fidelity QND measurement of the Pauli operator Z4 between the four nuclear spin qubits which in turn can be used to perform the parity checks re- quired for biased-noise error correction as in Fig. 2.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.24262_figures/2605.24262_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 4. High-fidelity QND readout of Z⊗3 between three sta- bilized cat qubits. As shown in plot (a), the setup consists of three dissipatively stabilized cat qubits coupled capacitively. Each cat qubit mode aj (in blue) is coupled to its buffer mode bj (in red). The Asymmetrically Threaded SQUIDs (ATS), represented by SQUIDs shunted in the middle by an induc- tance, ensure the nonlinear interactions between the cat qubit modes and the buffer modes. By applying dc magnetic fluxes of 0 and π in the two superconducting loops of the ATS, this element engineers a potential of the form (6). The j’th ATS is pumped with an ac drive at frequency 2ωaj −ωbj, ensuring effectively an exchange of two...</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.24262_figures/2605.24262_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> FIG. 5. Logical error rate per syndrome cycle of the XZZX surface code implemented with MZ4 measurements, as a function of dz. Top row: |+⟩memory (X-type logical). Bottom row: |0⟩memory (Z-type logical). Columns correspond to bias η ∈{10, 100, 103}. Colors indicate phase-flip rate pz (blue: 10−3; orange: 3×10−3; red: 5×10−3); solid and dashed lines correspond to dx = 3 and dx = 5, respectively. Downward triangles (▽) mark 95% Poisson upper bounds for tasks with zero observed errors. Thin lines show the power-law ansätze of Eqs. (11)–(B2).</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** The paper proposes using multi-qubit QND Z-measurements as a practical alternative to difficult-to-implement bias-preserving CNOT gates. This approach allows for highly efficient error correction in platforms with biased noise, such as cat qubits and spin-based systems, significantly reducing the required qubit overhead.

**Why it may be interesting.** This is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and quantum optics as it demonstrates how engineered dissipation and non-demolition measurements can be used as a fundamental building block for scalable, error-protected quantum architectures.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Existing approaches to exploit noise bias in fault-tolerant quantum computing rely on bias-preserving CNOT gates, which are experimentally difficult to implement and impossible in strictly 2D systems. The goal is to find a more accessible primitive that achieves similar hardware efficiency.

**Main result.** High-fidelity Quantum Non-Demolition (QND) multi-qubit Pauli Z measurements can replace bias-preserving CNOT gates, providing up to a 6x reduction in qubit overhead for XZZX surface codes at high noise bias. The scheme achieves phase-flip thresholds of approximately 1.25% for XZZX codes and 2.3% for repetition codes.

**Method.** The authors use circuit-level numerical simulations (via Stim and PyMatching) to evaluate error thresholds and qubit overhead. They also propose physical implementation protocols for multi-qubit Z-parity measurements using controlled-U operations and engineered dissipation.

**Model / system.** The paper targets platforms with biased noise (phase-flip > bit-flip), specifically solid-state nuclear spins coupled to electron spin ancillas and superconducting cat qubits stabilized via two-photon dissipation.

**Key observables.** Logical error rate (LER), phase-flip threshold, qubit overhead, and measurement assignment error.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Noise bias (eta) ranging from 10 to 10^8, phase-flip error rate (pz), and measurement weight (e.g., weight-4).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The assignment error of the QND measurement must be significantly smaller than the phase-flip error rate to preserve the noise bias.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the QND Z-measurement mechanism; Figure 2 shows a CNOT implementation using Z-measurements; Figure 4 illustrates the setup for cat qubits; Figures 15-19 detail XZZX surface code schedules and error rate comparisons.

**Paper structure.** The paper identifies the bottleneck of bias-preserving gates, proposes QND Z-measurements as a replacement, provides physical implementation paths for spin and superconducting systems, and quantifies performance gains through large-scale simulations.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Leveraging noise bias, where phase-flip errors dominate over bit-flips, can drastically reduce the hardware overhead of fault-tolerant quantum computation, but existing approaches require bias-preserving CNOT gates whose implementation remains experimentally challenging and is provably impossible for strictly two-dimensional systems. We show that high-fidelity quantum non-demolition (QND) multi-qubit Pauli $Z$ measurements provide an equally powerful yet more accessible primitive. We demonstrate that such measurements can fully replace bias-preserving CNOT gates for compiling all operations required by bias-tailored error correction, including stabilizer measurements for repetition codes, XZZX surface codes, and LDPC codes. We propose concrete physical implementations of this primitive for two platforms: solid-state nuclear spins coupled to electron spin ancillas, and dissipatively stabilized superconducting cat qubits. Through circuit-level numerical simulations, we show that an asymmetric XZZX surface code implemented with weight-four QND $Z$ measurements achieves a phase-flip threshold of $\sim\!1.25\%$ and provides a qubit overhead reduction of up to $6\times$ compared to a bias-unaware surface code at noise bias $η= 10^4$. In the regime of very large bias, a repetition code with QND $Z$ measurements attains a threshold of $\sim\!2.3\%$ and achieves overhead comparable to that of a bias-preserving CNOT scheme, without requiring such a gate. Our results establish QND multi-$Z$ measurements as a practical and hardware-efficient route to fault-tolerant quantum computation for a broad class of biased-noise platforms.

</details>

### [Structural constraint on delayed-choice quantum eraser architectures](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24994v1)

**Authors:** Chakir Fikri  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.24994v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `quantum measurements` **4/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.24994_figures/2605.24994_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 2. Example of architecture II.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.24994_figures/2605.24994_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 1. Example of architecture I.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.24994_figures/2605.24994_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 3. Example of architecture III.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that the seemingly paradoxical correlations in delayed-choice quantum eraser experiments are actually a result of incompatible structural assumptions. It proves that any realistic implementation must necessarily involve losses, non-deterministic routing, or coarse-graining to remain consistent with the independence of the measurement choice.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a fundamental structural explanation for the 'paradoxical' nature of DCQE, showing that losses are not just experimental flaws but a structural necessity to maintain causality and independence.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the apparent paradox in Delayed-Choice Quantum Eraser (DCQE) experiments, where measurement choices at a later time seem to correlate with earlier detection events.

**Main result.** The author proves a no-go theorem stating that four intuitive properties of idealized DCQE architectures—independence of choice, absence of losses, deterministic routing, and distinct conditional distributions—are mutually incompatible.

**Method.** A purely probabilistic analysis using elementary probability constraints and a framework of minimal compatibility relations between random variables, independent of specific quantum dynamics.

**Model / system.** A generic bipartite DCQE scenario involving a signal system (S) and an idler system (I), modeled via random variables for detection outcomes, experimental choices, and density operators.

**Key observables.** Detection positions (X), experimental settings (C), coincidence counts, and conditional detection distributions P(X|D).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Loss rate (p), probability of erasure choice (q), and the minimum loss rate threshold p_min = q^2/2.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The core derivation relies on the assumption of statistical independence between the choice and the signal detection (X is independent of C).

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates Architecture I (coarse-graining leading to interference cancellation); Figure 2 illustrates Architecture II (non-deterministic routing in a Mach-Zehnder setup).

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the DCQE paradox, defines a probabilistic model for the system, presents a no-go theorem for idealized architectures, classifies DCQE schemes into three types based on which property they violate, and provides an analytical derivation of loss rate constraints.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Delayed-choice quantum eraser (DCQE) experiments are often presented as challenging classical causal intuitions by correlating detection events with choices implemented at later times. While it is well understood that post-selection plays a crucial role in producing the observed interference patterns, the structural features underlying such correlations are rarely analyzed within a unified framework. In this work, we introduce a simple probabilistic constraint applicable to idealized DCQE architectures. We show that four intuitive properties -- statistical independence of the choice, absence of losses, deterministic routing conditioned on the choice, and distinct conditional detection distributions -- cannot be simultaneously satisfied. This incompatibility provides a transparent classification of DCQE schemes and clarifies how conditional interference patterns arise without invoking exotic mechanisms.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper presents a hybrid quantum-classical framework that uses a quantum processor to sample important electronic configurations and a Transformer neural network to refine the resulting wavefunction. It successfully demonstrates high-accuracy simulations of large, strongly correlated biological clusters that are challenging for classical computers.

**Why it may be interesting.** It demonstrates a powerful synergy between quantum hardware-native sampling and classical machine learning to overcome the limitations of NISQ noise and the exponential scaling of many-body wavefunctions.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Accurately simulating strongly correlated electronic structures on NISQ-era quantum hardware, which is hindered by noise, high measurement costs, and the exponential growth of the Hilbert space.

**Main result.** The QiankunNet-QSCI framework achieves chemical accuracy for a 40-qubit ferredoxin cluster and reaches 12-18 milli-Hartree agreement with DMRG for a massive 146-spin-orbital nitrogenase P-cluster.

**Method.** A hybrid quantum-classical approach using a Unitary Selected Configuration Interaction (USCI) ansatz for importance sampling on a quantum processor, followed by a Transformer-based neural network (QiankunNet) for denoising and wavefunction refinement.

**Model / system.** The study utilizes the Zuchongzhi 3.1 superconducting quantum processor to simulate various systems, including the H10 ring, the [2Fe-2S] ferredoxin active center, and the large-scale nitrogenase P-cluster (CAS(114e, 73o) active space).

**Key observables.** Ground-state energy, absolute energy error, excitation rank, single-orbital entropy, mutual information, and sampling enrichment factor.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Gate error rates (single-qubit ~0.1%, two-qubit ~0.6%), readout error (~0.6%), and the number of shots (up to 10^8).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The framework assumes a global depolarizing noise model and focuses on the importance of the chemically significant determinant sector.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the workflow stages (Quantum, Data, AI); Figure 2 validates the H10 ring; Figure 4c compares energy errors against classical methods and DMRG; supplementary figures show hardware metrics, circuit depths, and sampling distributions.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the scientific challenge, details the USCI quantum sampling and QiankunNet Transformer architecture, presents experimental implementation on Zuchongzhi 3.1, demonstrates scalability through various molecular benchmarks, and concludes with error analysis and future outlook.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Although quantum computing offers a promising solution for strongly correlated system simulation, existing algorithms face significant bottlenecks on current noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) devices. Here, we introduce QiankunNet-QSCI, a hybrid quantum-classical framework that addresses this challenge by combining efficient quantum-sampling with a transformer neural network. An efficient unitary selected configuration Interaction (USCI) ansatz especially designed for quantum sampling is proposed to identify the most chemically significant electronic configurations on the Zuchongzhi 3.1 quantum processor. Subsequently, the transformer model QiankunNet learns from these sparse yet critical quantum data to infer and reconstruct the complete electronic wavefunction with high fidelity. Simulation of the challenging 40-qubit [2Fe-2S] ferredoxin active center achieves chemical accuracy. Simulation of the nitrogenase P-cluster in a 114-electron 73-orbital active space also reaches 12 milli-Hartree-level agreement with the best density matrix renormalization group (DMRG) result. QiankunNet-QSCI thus offers a practical route to accurate quantum-assisted electronic structure calculations on current devices.

</details>

**Authors:** Esra Yeniaras  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.25066v1>

At the Corollary 1 (sampled-reference) per-observable budget nO = 2,280 (total N = 13,680), TPR reaches 1.00 and FPR drops to 0.00, comfortably bracketing the target rates (TPR target 1 −η = 0.95, FPR target η = 0.05, shown as dashed lines). At reduced budgets the FPR climbs sharply (0.15 at nO/10, 1.00 at nO/100), indicating that statistical fluctuations rather than channel deviation are driving the verifier’s decisions; the apparent...</figcaption>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a framework to verify that a quantum machine learning pipeline is executing the intended circuit rather than a substituted or degraded version. It provides a unified way to monitor for both malicious attacks and natural hardware noise using a lightweight measurement protocol. This is crucial for the secure deployment of QML in regulated cloud-based quantum services.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Ensuring the integrity of Quantum Machine Learning (QML) pipelines against both adversarial channel substitution and natural hardware calibration drift.

**Main result.** The QML-PipeGuard framework successfully detects 'sneaky' channel substitutions on IBM Heron hardware while distinguishing them from benign hardware drift within a calibrated tolerance.

**Method.** A contract-based framework using behavioral fingerprinting, where a vector of observable expectation values is compared against a declared specification using a predefined tolerance.

**Model / system.** The study focuses on QML pipelines (VQC, QSVM, QNN) implemented on the IBM Heron r2 processor (ibm_fez), modeling the quantum stage as a sequence of CPTP channels.

**Key observables.** A vector of observable expectation values, specifically using Pauli observable families (local Pauli, diagonal correlations, and full Pauli strings).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Calibrated tolerance (epsilon_A), adversarial separation (delta), frame-bound constant (C), and measurement shot budget (N).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes the adversary controls the execution environment, the QML model is already trained, and the verifier possesses a trusted reference implementation.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the system model of trust between owner, environment, and verifier; Figure 4 visualizes the relationship between shot budget and error rates (TPR/FPR).

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces a threat model for QML integrity, proposes the QML-PipeGuard framework, provides mathematical theorems for detection and sample complexity, and validates the approach via hardware experiments on IBM processors.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum machine learning (QML) is moving from research prototypes to deployed cloud services. As QML enters regulated industries, the integrity of the quantum stage becomes a practical concern on two fronts: noisy hardware drifts at the channel level between recalibrations, and an adversary with control over the execution environment can substitute the declared quantum channel with a behaviorally similar but mathematically distinct one. Neither concern is covered by existing QML verification work on pulse-level noise, input drift, input-perturbation robustness, or device identity. We introduce QML-PipeGuard, a contract-based framework addressing both concerns under a single mathematical machinery. It characterizes a QML pipeline at runtime by its behavioral fingerprint, the vector of observable expectation values under a tomographically structured measurement family, and operates in two modes: drift-aware monitoring that absorbs benign calibration changes within a calibrated tolerance, and adversarial detection that catches channel substitution as a violation of an informationally complete observable contract. The framework contributes a pipeline-composition treatment of the encoder-ansatz-measurement channel with a QML-specific threat model (tight frame-bound C=sqrt(3) for the single-qubit Pauli family), a finite-shot sample-complexity bound, and a tolerance decomposition separating adversarial and natural-drift contributions. We validate the framework end-to-end on a two-qubit QSVM pipeline on the IBM Heron r2 processor (ibm_fez), with a sample-complexity validation on a noise-matched simulator. The prescribed measurement budget (about 1.4e4 shots) fits in a single batched job, the sneaky channel is detected with a wide safety margin while evading the weak contract, and the typical hardware drift sits within tolerance.

</details>

### [Kernel Embedding for Operator-Valued Measures and Its Application to Quantum Tomography](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25146v1)

**Authors:** Philipp Nikolas Mayer, Ho Yun  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.25146v1>

**Summary.** This paper develops a new kernel-based framework for quantum state tomography that avoids the need for sparsity assumptions. By embedding measurements into a Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Space, the authors create a method that is statistically optimal and accounts for the physical geometry of the measurement apparatus.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a rigorous statistical foundation for quantum tomography that is robust to noise and does not rely on the potentially biased assumption of Pauli sparsity, making it highly relevant for characterizing realistic, noisy quantum hardware.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the limitations of existing Quantum State Tomography (QST) methods, which often rely on basis-dependent sparsity constraints that can introduce bias in the dense regime and fail to account for the spectral geometry of physical hardware.

**Main result.** The authors introduce the Quantum Covariance Embedding (QCE) and the QUARK estimator, proving they achieve minimax optimal rates for density estimation and establishing that Unitary Designs are statistically superior to Pauli observables.

**Method.** The framework utilizes Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Space (RKHS) techniques, specifically a tensorized Bochner integral, to embed Positive Operator-Valued Measures (POVMs) into a feature space, combined with kernel regression.

**Model / system.** The study focuses on finite-dimensional quantum systems (qubits and qudits) described by density operators in a Hilbert space, using measurement models ranging from ideal PVMs to noisy POVMs.

**Key observables.** Positive Operator-Valued Measures (POVMs), Pauli matrices, and Unitary Designs.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Dimension of the Hilbert space (q), number of measurement shots (r), error rate (eta) for bit-flip noise, and kernel bandwidth (c).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the use of characteristic kernels and focuses on finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces; it also assumes the existence of complete measurement designs for identifiability.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates the QCE pipeline from microscopic states to macroscopic outcomes; Figure 7 shows spectral error comparisons between LSE and QUARK; Figure 8 plots the MSE decay of various estimators.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining the mathematical framework for operator-valued kernel embeddings, moves to the construction of the Quantum Maximum Discrepancy metric, develops the QUARK estimator and its statistical properties (CLT, concentration inequalities), and concludes with numerical simulations comparing the new method to standard least-squares estimation.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

This paper introduces the Quantum Covariance Embedding, which embeds Positive Operator-Valued Measures into a tensor product of a Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Space and the quantum state space via a tensorized Bochner integral. This construction induces the Quantum Maximum Discrepancy that metrizes the space of quantum measurements. Applying this framework to Quantum State Tomography, we reformulate density estimation as a tensorized kernel regression, enabling optimal inference without the basis-dependent sparsity constraints that restrict existing methods. We develop a unified geometric design theory for quantum Gram superoperators, establishing that Unitary Designs are strictly E-optimal experimental designs and thus statistically superior to Pauli observables. For general structure-free estimation, we derive the exact minimax lower bound and prove that our tensorized estimators achieve this optimal rate. Furthermore, we introduce the QUAntum Regression with Kernels (QUARK) estimator to accommodate the spectral geometry of physical implementations, deriving central limit theorem and concentration inequalities. To facilitate practical estimation, we establish the exactness of trace-preserving projections and demonstrate efficient estimation under mutually unbiased bases via the fast Walsh-Hadamard transform.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper presents a new quantum-classical hybrid method to analyze the point-group symmetries of many-electron wavefunctions without requiring ancilla qubits. By applying orbital rotations, the method efficiently calculates the weights of irreducible representations. The approach is successfully demonstrated on real IBM quantum hardware, showing that error mitigation can accurately recover symmetry properties in molecular simulations.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a practical framework for using molecular symmetries to reduce computational overhead and verify the accuracy of quantum simulations on near-term hardware.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The lack of practical, ancilla-free quantum algorithms for performing detailed point-group symmetry analysis of realistic many-electron wavefunctions.

**Main result.** The authors propose an ancilla-free hybrid method to calculate irreducible representation weights and demonstrate its efficacy on IBM's ibm_kawasaki device, achieving results within a few percent error using QESEM error mitigation.

**Method.** A quantum-classical hybrid approach using orbital rotations derived from representation matrix eigenvectors, combined with tensor-network-based state preparation (DMRG-to-brick-wall) and error mitigation techniques like ZNE and QESEM.

**Model / system.** Many-electron molecular systems, specifically benzene (D6h) and ferrocene (D5d), simulated using electronic structure models and implemented on a superconducting quantum processor.

**Key observables.** Weights of irreducible representations (w_Gamma), energy expectation values, and wavefunction infidelity.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Number of qubits (up to 32), number of circuit layers (L=6), regularization parameter (lambda = 0.005), and error mitigation strategies (ZNE, QESEM).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study focuses on the spin-restricted case and utilizes DMRG-compressed wavefunctions for the hardware demonstration.

**Figures summary.** Figures show the optimization process for brick-wall ansatz construction, infidelity vs. circuit layers, and a comparison of symmetry weight estimation accuracy under different error mitigation regimes (No mitigation, ZNE, and QESEM).

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of symmetry analysis, proposes a new ancilla-free algorithm, validates it through numerical simulations of benzene and ferrocene, and concludes with a hardware demonstration on an IBM quantum device.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

A point group is a set of spatial symmetry operations in molecular systems and is an indispensable tool for analyzing molecular orbitals and spectroscopy experiments in chemistry. Several quantum algorithms to exploit this symmetry have been proposed, but practical implementations of point-group symmetry operations and the detailed symmetry analysis of realistic many-electron wavefunctions are still missing. In this work, we propose an ancilla-free hybrid method to analyze point-group symmetries of many-electron states, which works for both abelian and non-abelian groups. For a given wavefunction, our method calculates the projection weights of point-group irreducible representations by applying orbital rotations derived from the eigenvectors of the representation matrices, therefore it is applicable to arbitrary basis functions. The usefulness of our approach is demonstrated through numerical simulations of benzene and ferrocene molecules. Furthermore, we perform a hardware demonstration of the weight calculation of the ground state and the first excited state of benzene in $D_{2h}$ symmetry, using up to 32 qubits of IBM's \texttt{ibm\_kawasaki} device. By combining a tensor-network based encoding scheme and error mitigation techniques, we find the weights of irreducible representations for both states are faithfully reproduced within a few percent error. Our results suggest that the proposed method serves as a practical tool for analyzing symmetry properties of many-electron wavefunctions in realistic material simulations on near-term and early fault-tolerant quantum computers.

</details>
