**Summary.** This paper develops a mathematical framework for applying geometric quantization to spaces with singularities, such as orbifolds. It specifically resolves how to handle the lack of equivariant half-forms in systems with reflection symmetry by using half-densities, ensuring correct quantum spectra.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Extending the framework of geometric quantization from smooth manifolds to orbifolds, specifically addressing challenges like quotient singularities, metaplectic correction obstructions, and the failure of Kähler polarizations in the presence of reflections.

**Main result.** The paper establishes a unified treatment of geometric quantization on orbifolds, demonstrating that while genuine half-form bundles may be obstructed by reflection isotropy, the use of half-densities can restore physical consistency and correct energy spectra.

**Method.** The author uses the Kostant-Souriau prequantization framework, applying tools from orbifold theory, de Rham cohomology, and metaplectic (half-form) correction to analyze specific symplectic orbifolds.

**Model / system.** The study focuses on symplectic orbifolds including planar cones, orbispheres (S2(n, m)), and the cotangent bundle of a dihedral quotient (T*(C/Dn)).

**Key observables.** Hamiltonian vector fields, Poisson brackets, commutator relations, and position-based observables like radial position (rho^2) and kinetic energy.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Planck's constant (hbar), orbifold weights (n, m), and the Maslov index.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The paper assumes orbifolds with mild singularities (local group-quotient setting) and focuses on cases where the symplectic form is either exact or satisfies specific integrality conditions.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from a review of standard smooth manifold quantization to the development of orbifold-specific theory, followed by detailed analysis of geometric examples (orbispheres and dihedral quotients), investigation of quantum spectra, and a formal extension of the quantization program.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

This text introduces geometric quantization on orbifolds. After reviewing the necessary background, it develops new treatments of prequantization, polarizations, and metaplectic correction for symplectic orbifolds.

</details>

### [Homomorphic Quantum Error Correction](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25692v1)

**Authors:** Kornikar Sen, Miguel A. Martin-Delgado  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.25692v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

<img src="2605.25692_figures/2605.25692_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 2. Key updating rules for various Clifford gates. The way in which the keys get updated on the action of (top to bottom in the figure) X, Z, H, S, and CNOT gates is depicted in the above figure.</figcaption>
</figure>

<img src="2605.25692_figures/2605.25692_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">

The gray box represents the measurement performed on the two qubits that belong to the sender. Depending on the measurement outcome, a and b, the receiver operates the gates Xa (cyan box) and Zb (blue box) on the qubit that be- longs to the receiver and gets the state, U |ψ⟩.</figcaption>
</figure>

**Summary.** This paper investigates how to combine quantum error correction with homomorphic encryption to allow secure, noise-resilient computation in the cloud. It provides the mathematical conditions required to keep encrypted data within a protected code space and offers strategies to overcome the difficulty of performing non-Clifford gates on encrypted data.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Determining the algebraic compatibility between quantum homomorphic encryption (QHE) and quantum error correction (QEC) to ensure that encrypted states remain within the code space during cloud-based computation.

**Main result.** The authors establish a necessary and sufficient condition for stabilizer codes to be compatible with transversal Pauli masking and propose solutions for implementing non-Clifford gates, such as using triorthogonal codes or logical-gate masking.

**Method.** Algebraic analysis of stabilizer codes, verification using specific examples (Shor, Steane, and 3D color codes), and the development of gate teleportation protocols for non-Clifford gates.

**Model / system.** A client-server model for cloud quantum computing using an 'honest-but-curious' server, employing Pauli-based encryption (block-Pauli masking) and stabilizer error-correcting codes.

**Key observables.** Error syndromes, encryption keys (a, b), and measurement outcomes (ra, rb) from Bell measurements.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Small error rate regime (p << 1).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The server is honest-but-curious (not malicious); the existence of pre-shared Bell states; and the availability of physical implementations for logical gates.

**Figures summary.** Schematic of the gate teleportation process, visual representation of key-updating rules for Clifford gates, and a walkthrough of a two-qubit circuit involving H, T, T-dagger, and S gates.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of QHE and QEC compatibility, derives a general algebraic criterion for stabilizer codes, verifies this for specific codes (Shor, CSS, 3D color), addresses the non-Clifford gate obstruction, and presents solutions via triorthogonal codes and logical-gate masking.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Homomorphic quantum error correction aims to protect quantum data against both unauthorized access and environmental noise during server-based processing. We investigate the algebraic compatibility between quantum homomorphic encryption and quantum error correction, determining precise conditions under which encrypted encoded states remain inside the relevant code space during storage and computation. Our work establishes a necessary and sufficient criterion for an $[[n,1,d]]$ stabilizer code to remain compatible with the restricted transversal block-Pauli masking $U_{\rm enc}(a,b)=(X^aZ^b)^{\otimes n}$, stated explicitly for $[[n,1,d]]$ codes and extending directly to code-space preservation for $[[n,k,d]]$ codes. We verify this condition for standard examples (bit-flip and Shor codes, with the phase-flip repetition code following analogously), derive a practical criterion for Calderbank-Shor-Steane codes, and extend the analysis to three-dimensional color codes. A critical challenge emerges for non-Clifford gate implementation: the Shor code lacks a naive transversal $T$-gate implementation of the desired logical operation on encrypted encoded data. We present two routes around this obstruction. First, suitable triorthogonal codes admit transversal $T$-type logical implementations, up to Clifford corrections. Second, logical-gate masking gives code-space compatibility for arbitrary stabilizer codes, provided that suitable unitary representatives of the required logical gates are available. These results separate code-space compatibility from a full cryptographic security proof and provide explicit criteria for combining error correction with homomorphic processing in cloud quantum computing.

</details>

**Authors:** Khen Cohen, Mark Glass, Meir Feder, Yaron Oz  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.24502v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Averages were computed over 20 distinct random problem realizations, utilizing 20 trials with random initial conditions for each realization.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper proposes a new way to solve hard optimization problems by treating discrete bits as complex phases. This approach creates a natural 'push' toward discrete values, allowing for much more accurate solutions to complex problems like QUBO and sparse coding even under high noise.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Addressing the computational intractability of NP-hard combinatorial optimization problems, such as QUBO and sparse coding, by overcoming the limitations of standard continuous convex relaxations.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that parameterizing binary variables as complex phases introduces an implicit regularization mechanism that promotes convergence to discrete states, achieving zero error in large-scale noisy QUBO tasks and outperforming LASSO in sparse coding.

**Method.** A physics-inspired continuous relaxation framework that maps discrete binary variables to continuous wave-like states on the complex unit circle, utilizing gradient descent and analyzing the resulting implicit regularization via random matrix theory.

**Model / system.** The framework utilizes an Ising Hamiltonian formulation where discrete spins are relaxed into higher-dimensional algebraic manifolds, specifically the complex unit circle, spherical, and quaternion spaces.

**Key observables.** Ground-state convergence rates, bit error rates, and recovery success of planted solutions.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The diagonal shift hyperparameter beta, noise levels sigma, sparsity cardinality C, and the spectral properties (singular values) of the measurement matrix A.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The effectiveness of the binarization depends on the spectral characteristics of the interaction matrix, and for certain QUBO formulations, the measurement matrix is assumed to be square with determinant 1.

**Figures summary.** Visualizations of the continuous relaxation process across different manifolds (real, complex, spherical) and histograms of estimated ground-state energies.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the complex phase relaxation framework, provides analytical proofs for the implicit regularization and binarization basin, demonstrates the extraction of this regularizer for real-valued use, and validates the method through QUBO, sparse coding, and planted Ising model benchmarks.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We introduce a physics-inspired continuous relaxation framework that yields substantially improved solutions for NP-hard combinatorial optimization problems, including Quadratic Unconstrained Binary Optimization (QUBO), binary sparse coding, and planted-solution Ising models. By parameterizing discrete binary variables as continuous wave-like states on the complex unit circle, we inherently smooth highly non-convex energy landscapes. We show that representing binary variables as complex phases reveals an implicit regularization mechanism that promotes convergence toward discrete states. Extracting this mechanism yields significant improvements even within standard real-valued optimization frameworks, using this regularizer explicitly. Empirically, this regularization yields vastly higher ground-state convergence rates than standard real-valued alternatives. Our models achieved zero error in large-scale 160x160 QUBO tasks under severe noise (sigma=0.25), and outperformed traditional algorithms (OMP and LASSO) in underdefined sparse coding with perfect recovery at sigma=0.15. The solver's robustness was further validated by recovering exact ground-state configurations in 8 out of 11 rigorously engineered planted-solution benchmarks.

</details>

### [Improved Dual Attack and Trapdoor Sampling via Quantum Rejection Sampling](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24798v1)

**Authors:** Cong Ling, Hao Yan, Nicholas Zhao  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.24798v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

The first two gates AR = Ra(OQR ⊗I0) prepare the state for QRS. The operator UR is one iterate of the amplitude amplification subroutine, whose dashed box gives its full decomposition. This is repeated O  1 √pR</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.24798_figures/2605.24798_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Fig. 3: MCMC and QRS upper bounds derived from [29]’s ϑ3(γ)n estimate for FALCON-512. The horizontal axis uses the Gaussian parameter s, with γ = (s/Bmax)2.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a quantum algorithm to accelerate the discrete Gaussian sampling required for lattice-based cryptographic attacks. By using quantum rejection sampling, the authors achieve a quadratic speedup in the sampling process, leading to lower estimated security levels for post-quantum standards like Kyber.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The Gaussian sampling step in lattice-based cryptographic attacks (dual attack) and GPV trapdoor sampling is a significant computational bottleneck that remains classical.

**Main result.** The authors develop a quantum algorithm using Quantum Rejection Sampling (QRS) that achieves a quadratic speedup in sampling complexity, reducing the estimated attack costs for Kyber variants by up to 13 bits.

**Method.** The method combines the lower bound of Klein's algorithm with the Quantum Rejection Sampling framework to prepare truncated lattice Gaussian states using amplitude amplification.

**Model / system.** The work focuses on lattice-based cryptographic primitives, specifically the Learning with Errors (LWE) and Short Integer Solution (SIS) problems involving q-ary lattices and their duals.

**Key observables.** Attack cost estimates (in bits), total variation distance between truncated and full distributions, and the empirical cosine score for identifying secret vectors.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The Gaussian parameter (s), the truncation radius (R), the sampling ratio (Delta_R), and the Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization profile (Delta).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The algorithm assumes access to a coherent oracle for a truncated Klein proposal distribution and requires a truncation radius that ensures negligible error in total variation distance.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the concentration of discrete Gaussian mass for different Gaussian parameters; Figure 2 illustrates the oracle-level decomposition of the QRS circuit; Figure 3 shows the exponential gap between MCMC and QRS upper bounds.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the bottleneck in lattice sampling, proposes a QRS-based framework using a truncated Klein proposal, provides mathematical proofs for complexity reduction and error bounds, and evaluates the impact on Kyber and FALCON security parameters.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

In this work, we revisit the dual attack and GPV trapdoor sampling, focusing on the lattice Gaussian sampling term, which can be a significant bottleneck in the overall complexity. We show that this sampling step can be quantumly accelerated by combining the lower bound underlying Wang and Ling's analysis of Klein's algorithm with the quantum rejection sampling (QRS) framework proposed by Ozols et al. Specifically, this lower bound gives precisely the pointwise domination condition required for quantum rejection sampling when given coherent oracle access to a truncated Klein proposal distribution, which yields a quantum procedure for preparing the truncated dual $q$-ary lattice Gaussian with a quadratic reduction in the sampling complexity. The truncation radius is chosen so that the truncated distribution is negligibly close to the full lattice Gaussian in total variation distance. Substituting this sampler into the dual attack framework results in reduced overall attack-cost estimates. Compared with Pouly and Shen's modern dual attack under the same parameter choices, our estimates reduce the attack cost by \(9\), \(4\), and \(13\) bits for Kyber-512, Kyber-768, and Kyber-1024, respectively. We also report the corresponding estimates with modulus switching. Finally, by replacing the Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampler with the QRS algorithm, we achieve a similar quadratic speedup in the GPV signing process.

</details>

**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.25033v1>

</figure>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that shifting the legs of a quasiperiodic ladder relative to each other allows for the tuning of localization phases via a synthetic magnetic flux. It introduces a highly accurate, system-size-independent method for identifying phase transitions in quasiperiodic systems. The findings suggest a tunable platform for studying complex localization phenomena like reentrant transitions.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating how a lateral shift between two legs of a quasiperiodic ladder can act as a control knob to induce various localization-delocalization transitions.

**Main result.** The lateral shift effectively generates a synthetic magnetic flux in reciprocal space, enabling magnetic-flux-enhanced, suppressed, and reentrant localization transitions. The study also introduces a new, system-size-independent diagnostic called the anisotropic bandwidth ratio (Rj) to precisely identify phase boundaries.

**Method.** The authors use a duality transformation to map the real-space ladder to a reciprocal-space Hamiltonian and employ a commensurate approximation using Fibonacci numbers to analyze the band structure.

**Model / system.** A 1D quasiperiodic ladder where two legs are subject to the same quasiperiodic onsite potential but are laterally shifted relative to each other, effectively simulating a synthetic magnetic flux.

**Key observables.** Inverse Participation Ratio (IPR), Normalized Participation Ratio (NPR), Fractal Dimension (FD), eta diagnostic, and the anisotropic bandwidth ratio (Rj).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Lateral shift (q or phi), interleg coupling (kappa), quasiperiodic potential strength (v), and the inverse golden ratio (alpha).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The use of a commensurate approximation to treat the irrational quasiperiodic potential as a periodic system with a finite unit cell.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the schematic of the ladder and its duality-transformed version with synthetic flux. Fig 2/4 shows phase maps and logarithmic plots of the Rj diagnostic (Rmin and Rmax) used to delineate extended, mixed, and localized phases.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the quasiperiodic ladder model, establishes the duality between lateral shift and synthetic magnetic flux, presents numerical results for various localization transitions, introduces the Rj diagnostic for phase boundary prediction, and concludes with potential applications.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

This work reports rich localization-delocalization transitions in a quasiperiodic ladder, of which the two legs are subject to the same quasiperiodic onsite potential but can be shifted laterally relative to each other. It is found that the lateral shift between the two legs effectively generates a magnetic flux in the reciprocal momentum space. The lateral shift thus offers a control knob, allowing us to access and simulate rich phenomena including magnetic-flux-enhanced localization, magnetic-flux-suppressed localization, and magnetic-flux-induced reentrant localization transitions. The underlying physical mechanisms as well as the phase boundaries separating localized, mixed, and extended phases are both qualitatively and quantitatively understood, based on a band-structure analysis that employs a commensurate approximation to the quasiperiodic potential, requiring only unit cells of small to modest sizes. Our work provides a highly tunable platform for exploring localization physics with promising applications such as quantum switching, and a broadly applicable approach for understanding localization-delocalization transitions in quasiperiodic systems.

</details>

**Authors:** Leonie Woodland, Farid Labib, Dmitry Khalyavin, Pascal Manuel, Fabio Orlandi, Hubertus Luetkens, Ryuji Tamura  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.26044v1>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1. Powder neutron diffraction data on Cd6Tb. (a) Data at 30 K, 1.5 K and the fit to the 30 K data. (b) Magnetic diffraction pattern obtained by taking the difference between the 1.5 K and 30 K data in (a), together with the magnetic refinement (solid black line). The difference curve (blue) has been offset by 4 units for clar- ity.</figcaption>

(a,b) The refined mag- netic structures of the apical (z = 0, k2) spins (a) and equa- torial (k1) spins (b) in Cd6Tb. The black outlines in both panels indicate a 2 × 2 × 1 magnetic supercell. In (a), down spins are highlighted in yellow for clarity. (c) The measured (average) moment can be half of the full moment if one ran- domly chosen spin in every four is flipped.</figcaption>

**Summary.** This paper identifies a candidate for octahedral spin-ice physics in the quasicrystal approximant Cd6Tb. By combining neutron diffraction and muon spin rotation, the authors demonstrate that the magnetic ground state consists of both ordered and fluctuating spin components, a phenomenon known as magnetic fragmentation.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study aims to resolve the microscopic magnetic ground state of the Tsai-type quasicrystal approximant Cd6Tb and determine if it realizes magnetic fragmentation, a key prediction of octahedral spin-ice physics.

**Main result.** The researchers discovered a noncoplanar multi-k magnetic ground state where one-third of the Tb moments are ordered and two-thirds exhibit persistent spin dynamics, providing a potential realization of magnetic fragmentation.

**Method.** Neutron powder diffraction was used for magnetic structure refinement, while Muon Spin Rotation (muSR) and AC susceptibility were employed to investigate spin dynamics and distinguish between static and dynamic magnetism.

**Model / system.** The system is Cd6Tb, a 1/1-cubic quasicrystal approximant featuring a network of corner-sharing octahedra with Ising-like Tb3+ moments.

**Key observables.** Neutron diffraction patterns, magnetic propagation vectors (k1 and k2), magnetic moments (apical vs. equatorial), muSR asymmetry/relaxation rates, and AC susceptibility.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Magnetic ordering temperature (~20 K), persistent dynamics on microsecond timescales, and the reduction of equatorial moments to ~4.62 uB compared to apical moments of ~10.2 uB.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The structural refinement used a cubic model because the subtle monoclinic distortion could not be fully resolved in powder neutron diffraction; diffraction alone could not distinguish between certain relative phases of the magnetic structure.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows neutron diffraction data and structural fits; Fig 2 illustrates the refined magnetic structures for apical and equatorial spins and the spin-flip model; Fig 3 shows the frustrated Heisenberg exchange model.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of quasicrystal magnetism, describes the structural and magnetic characterization of Cd6Tb via neutron diffraction, presents the discovery of the multi-k ground state, uses muSR to prove the presence of persistent dynamics, and concludes with the identification of magnetic fragmentation.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Magnetic ordering in quasicrystals has recently emerged as a fertile ground for discovering unconventional magnetic states beyond the framework of periodic crystals. However, elucidating the microscopic origin of such states remains challenging due to the intrinsic aperiodicity of quasicrystals. Here, we address this issue by investigating the prototypical Tsai-type quasicrystal approximant Cd6Tb, which preserves the essential local geometry and connectivity of icosahedral quasicrystals while allowing detailed structural and magnetic characterization due to its translational periodicity. Using neutron diffraction measurements, we find a noncoplanar multi-k magnetic ground state composed of Ising-like Tb moments arranged on a network of corner-sharing octahedra, the ingredients required to host octahedral spin-ice physics. Remarkably, only one third of the Tb moments develop long-range magnetic order, whereas the remaining moments display strongly reduced static order accompanied by persistent spin dynamics on microsecond timescales, as evidenced by muon spin rotation. This coexistence of ordered and fluctuating moments constitutes a potential realization of magnetic fragmentation - a key prediction of octahedral spin-ice physics - in a quasicrystal-related material.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper uses ultrasound spectroscopy to reveal how lattice vibrations and magnetic order interact in the frustrated antiferromagnet CrB2. It demonstrates that the material uses both transverse and longitudinal magnetoelastic coupling to manage magnetic frustration and Fermi-surface instabilities. These findings provide insight into the complex magnetostructural dynamics in metallic frustrated systems.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating the interplay between lattice and magnetic degrees of freedom in the metallic frustrated antiferromagnet CrB2, specifically how magnetoelastic coupling affects magnetic frustration and Fermi-surface nesting.

**Main result.** The study identifies that transverse magnetoelastic coupling leads to Curie-type softening in the shear modulus C66 as a precursor to a spin-Jahn-Teller transition, while longitudinal coupling suppresses Fermi-surface nesting.

**Method.** Ultrasound velocity measurements using the phase-comparison technique on high-quality single crystals of CrB2.

**Model / system.** The physical system is CrB2, a hexagonal metallic frustrated antiferromagnet with a Cr triangular lattice and a Neel temperature of approximately 88 K.

**Key observables.** Elastic moduli (C11, C33, C44, C66), sound velocities (longitudinal and transverse), and lattice parameter ratio (c/a).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Neel temperature TN ~ 88 K, frustration ratio |thetaW/TN| ~ 8.5, and temperature range from 2 K to 300 K.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the Curie-type softening can be modeled using a mean-field expression and that conventional elastic hardening follows the Varshni equation.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the CrB2 unit cell and sound propagation vectors; Figure 2 displays the temperature dependence of compressive moduli C11 and C33; Figure 3 shows the temperature dependence of shear moduli C44 and C66; Figure 5 highlights the relative variations in different moduli; Figure 6 shows the c/a ratio temperature dependence.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the material properties, describes the experimental ultrasound setup, presents the temperature dependence of various elastic moduli, analyzes the mechanisms of softening (spin-JT vs. FS nesting), and concludes with the dual role of magnetoelastic coupling.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Hexagonal chromium diboride CrB$_2$ is a metallic frustrated antiferromagnet with a Néel temperature $T_N \sim$ 88 K. In CrB$_2$, Cr 3$d$ electrons not only give rise to localized magnetic moments but also contribute to metallic conduction. We perform ultrasound velocity measurements on a single crystal of hexagonal CrB$_2$ to determine its elastic properties. The temperature dependence of the $ab$-plane shear elastic modulus exhibits Curie-type softening upon cooling from $\sim$120 K down to $T_N$. This behavior is interpreted as a precursor to a symmetry-lowering lattice distortion at $T_N$, indicating that magnetic frustration is relieved via transverse magnetoelastic coupling. In addition, the $a$-axis and $c$-axis compressive elastic moduli show unusual softness and their suppression upon cooling, which are naturally explained by Fermi-surface nesting and its suppression upon cooling. The present results suggest that, in CrB$_2$, longitudinal magnetoelastic coupling suppresses Fermi-surface nesting and enhances frustrated exchange interactions, while transverse magnetoelastic coupling plays a key role in relieving the frustration.

</details>

**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.24628v1>

**Summary.** This paper establishes a fundamental trade-off between memory capability and state-space fluctuations in recurrent neural networks, termed a memory uncertainty relation. It introduces the concept of harmonic memory as a realizable lower bound and demonstrates a phenomenon where input noise can actually enhance memory capacity under regularization.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Establishing a fundamental lower bound on the short-term memory capability of dynamical systems and investigating the relationship between memory capacity and state-space fluctuations.

**Main result.** The authors prove a 'memory uncertainty relation' where the memory function is bounded below by a 'harmonic memory' function, and discover that noise can induce increased memory capacity under certain regularization conditions.

**Method.** Analytical derivation using Jensen's inequality, the Cramér-Rao inequality, and the AM-HM inequality, supplemented by numerical simulations of various reservoir architectures.

**Model / system.** Random recurrent networks, specifically Echo State Networks (ESNs) including Distributed Shift Register Networks (DSRNs), Random Orthogonal Networks (RONs), and Random Gaussian Networks (RGNs).

**Key observables.** Memory function m(k), harmonic memory h(k), memory capacity MC, and fluctuation measure f(k).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Noise strength (beta), spectral radius (alpha), regularization threshold (epsilon), and network size (N).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The covariance matrix is positive definite, the input signal is stationary with mean 0 and variance 1, and certain results rely on the large N limit.

**Figures summary.** Figures show the decay of memory functions m(k) and h(k) for different network types, the difference between them, and the effect of noise strength on regularized memory capacity.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the memory uncertainty relation, provides analytical proofs for the lower bound, examines specific network architectures (DSRN, RON, RGN), explores the effect of state-space regularization (PCR), and analyzes finite-time effects and error scaling.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We present an inequality that bounds the short-term memory capability of dynamical systems from below. It can be interpreted as an uncertainty relation between a measure of short-term memory and that of the size of state fluctuations induced by input signals. The lower bound can be achieved by a readout weight and thus represents a suboptimal memory called harmonic memory. We examine analytically and numerically the inequality in a number of reservoir systems subject to input noise. We illustrate cases in which equality is achieved exactly, equality holds asymptotically, and the inequality is strict. We also study the effect of a state-space regularization to elucidate the inequality in terms of the fluctuation structure of the state-space. We find that a certain strength of input noise induces extra memory under the regularization, and we refer to this phenomenon as noise-induced memory. We observe that the memory uncertainty relation does not hold in general for the regularized memory and harmonic memory. This fact is explained in terms of the mechanism of noise-induced memory.

</details>

**Authors:** Subham Naik, Biswajit Singh, Hiranmayee Senapati, Akshay K. U., Ramesh C. Nath, Soumyojit Chatterjee, Rahul Sharma, Thomas Doert, Walter Schnelle, Manfred Reehuis, Thomas C. Hansen, Michael Ruck, Gohil S. Thakur

**Summary.** This paper reports the discovery of near-room-temperature antiferromagnetic ordering in the quadruple perovskite Sr4NaRu3O12. By combining neutron diffraction and DFT, the authors reveal a unique magnetic structure with frustrated Ru sites and a semiconducting electronic state. This work provides a new platform for studying magnetic exchange pathways in 4d-metal oxides.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study aims to synthesize and characterize a new 1:3 ordered quadruple perovskite, Sr4NaRu3O12, to explore its structural, electronic, and magnetic properties, specifically looking for well-defined magnetic order in 4d transition-metal oxides.

**Main result.** The researchers discovered near-room-temperature antiferromagnetic ordering (TN ~ 265 K) in Sr4NaRu3O12, which is a narrow-gap semiconductor with a specific collinear spin alignment and magnetic frustration at certain Ru sites.

**Method.** The work combines experimental techniques including solid-state synthesis, single-crystal and powder X-ray/neutron diffraction, magnetometry (SQUID/VSM), and transport measurements with computational DFT (Quantum Espresso) calculations.

**Model / system.** The primary system is the 12R quadruple perovskite Sr4NaRu3O12, characterized by a rhombohedral structure with corner-connected RuO6 and NaO6 octahedra and Ru in a +5 oxidation state.

**Key observables.** Néel temperature (TN), magnetic propagation vector (k = 0, 0, 1.5), magnetic moments (mu_eff), electrical resistivity, and electronic band gap.

**Important parameters / regimes.** TN ~ 265 K, Weiss constant (theta_cw) ~ -540 K, band gap ~ 250 meV, and Ru oxidation state +5.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The structural refinement assumes a centrosymmetric space group R-3; DFT calculations utilize the GGA-PBE approximation with a Hubbard U of 2.0 eV.

**Figures summary.** Figure 8 shows neutron diffraction difference patterns and magnetic peak intensity temperature dependence alongside a schematic of the spin sequence; Figure 9 displays the calculated electronic band structure.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the motivation for studying 4d oxides, details the synthesis and structural characterization of the Na and Li analogues, presents magnetic and transport measurements, provides DFT-based electronic structure analysis, and concludes with a comparison of the two compounds.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We report the synthesis, structure and magnetic properties of two 1:3 ordered quadruple perovskites Sr4MRu3O12 (M = Li and Na). Sr4NaRu3O12 crystallizes in the centrosymmetric space group R-3 and Sr4LiRu3O12 appears to be isostructural to the Na compound based on the PXRD data. In Sr4NaRu3O12, both Na and Ru are predominantly ordered at the B sites (here Na/Li and Ru) and the structure contains only corner-connected RuO6 and NaO6 octahedra. This atomic ordering also leads to a rather large unit cell with a = 11.25 Å and c = 27.6 Å compared to the basic 12R structure (a = 5.5 Å and c ~ 27 Å). Magnetic measurements reveal that Sr4NaRu3O12 undergoes a magnetic transition to an antiferromagnetic state below TN ~ 265 K which is confirmed by DSC and neutron diffraction. The Ru moments show a collinear antiferromagnetic spin alignment along the hexagonal c axis with a propagation vector k = (0, 0, 1.5). Interestingly, those Ru moments lying on the three-fold roto-inversion do not significantly contribute to the magnetic order, since they are located between antiferromagnetically coupled Ru atoms and are therefore probably highly frustrated. Band structure calculations on Sr4NaRu3O12 complement the observed magnetic ground state and a semiconducting behavior in the compound. Sr4LiRu3O12 shows a magnetic anomaly below 110 K, possibly associated with competing ferromagnetic and antiferromagnetic interactions.

</details>

(c) Magnetic-field dependence of 𝜅𝑥𝑥/𝑇 above 2 K, showing an overall enhancement with...</figcaption>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that in a strongly disordered triangular Mott magnet, charge-neutral excitations can remain mobile and carry heat even when charge transport is suppressed. This challenges the standard view that strong disorder inevitably leads to the localization of all low-energy excitations via Anderson-like mechanisms.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Determining whether low-energy, charge-neutral excitations can remain itinerant in a highly disordered, frustrated triangular Mott insulator, or if disorder-induced localization prevails.

**Main result.** The discovery of a finite, field-independent residual thermal conductivity in Cr3Se2Br5, providing evidence for itinerant, charge-neutral excitations that persist despite strong intrinsic structural disorder.

**Method.** The study uses a combination of structural analysis (XRD, electron diffraction), magnetic probes (susceptibility, TRM), and low-temperature transport measurements (thermal conductivity, specific heat).

**Model / system.** The system is Cr3Se2Br5, a van der Waals-based S = 3/2 Mott insulator with a frustrated triangular lattice and significant intrinsic disorder due to Cr-site deficiency and Se/Br-site mixing.

**Key observables.** Thermal conductivity (kappa_xx/T), magnetic specific heat (C_mag/T), magnetic susceptibility, and optical absorption gap.

**Important parameters / regimes.** S = 3/2 spin state, charge gap of ~1.35 eV, residual thermal conductivity kappa_0/T approx 0.03 W m^-1 K^-2, and exchange interaction J approx 0.22 meV.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The disorder is assumed to be intrinsic and fixed due to charge balance requirements, and the thermal transport is assumed to be decoupled from the localized thermodynamic spectral weight.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows structural and electronic characterization, including XRD patterns, electron diffraction, the crystal structure model with site disorder, and the optical absorption spectrum.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with structural and electronic characterization, moves to magnetic properties to rule out conventional order or glassiness, presents thermodynamic and thermal transport data to identify itinerant excitations, and concludes with a discussion on the interplay of disorder and correlations.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Understanding if low-energy excitations can remain itinerant in the presence of strong disorder remains a central challenge in frustrated quantum magnets, where disorder is generally expected to localize excitations through Anderson-like mechanisms. Here we report the emergence of charge-neutral itinerant excitations in a van der Waals compound Cr$_3$Se$_2$Br$_5$, a strongly disordered $S = 3/2$ Mott insulator with a frustrated triangular lattice. Structural analysis reveals substantial intrinsic disorder arising from Cr-site deficiency and Se/Br-site mixing, which appear to be fixed and cannot be readily tuned. No long-range magnetic order or conventional glassy behavior is observed. In addition to its highly insulating nature, the magnetic specific heat C_mag/T and thermal conductivity \k{appa}_xx/T both exhibit linear temperature dependencies with substantial finite intercepts. In particular, a sizeable field-independent residual term $κ/T \approx 0.03~\mathrm{W\,m^{-1}\,K^{-2}}$ is observed, providing compelling evidence of itinerant low-energy excitations that carry entropy without charge. These findings conceptually advance our understanding of quantum matter by demonstrating a rare regime where the interplay of disorder, frustration, and electronic correlations actively reshapes the nature of low-energy excitations, allowing itinerant neutral excitations to coexist with strong intrinsic disorder.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper resolves how a superconductor can break time-reversal symmetry while appearing fully gapped by demonstrating that the nodes of a nonunitary triplet state lie in empty regions of momentum space. It establishes SrPd2As2 as a significant platform for studying intrinsic topological superconductivity.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Resolving the contradiction in SrPd2As2 between experimental evidence of time-reversal symmetry breaking and thermodynamic probes that indicate a fully gapped superconducting state.

**Main result.** The study identifies a nonunitary triplet superconducting state where symmetry-imposed nodes are located in momentum-space regions devoid of electronic states, allowing the system to appear fully gapped despite breaking time-reversal symmetry.

**Method.** The researchers combined muon spin rotation and relaxation (muSR) experiments with first-principles DFT calculations, Wannier-based tight-binding models, and Ginzburg-Landau symmetry analysis.

**Model / system.** The physical system is SrPd2As2, a Z2 topological metal with a ThCr2Si2-type body-centered tetragonal structure, characterized by strong spin-orbit coupling and an open Fermi surface topology.

**Key observables.** Transition temperature (Tc), spontaneous internal magnetic fields, superconducting gap structure, Z2 topological invariant, and magnetic penetration depth.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Tc = 0.94 K, Z2 invariant = (1; 111), gap ratio 2Delta(0)/kBTc = 3.13, and electron-phonon coupling constant lambda = 0.65.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the material's symmetry-imposed nodes do not intersect the Fermi surface, and uses the Migdal-Eliashberg framework to test phonon-mediated pairing.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the unit cell, electronic band structure, and Fermi surface; Figure 2 displays muSR spectra and PCA scores; Figure 6 shows the XRD pattern; Figure 7 presents phonon dispersion and the Eliashberg spectral function.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the material and the scientific contradiction, presents experimental muSR evidence for TRS breaking and a fully gapped state, details the computational electronic structure and topological analysis, uses Ginzburg-Landau and Migdal-Eliashberg theory to propose a nonunitary triplet state, and concludes by validating this model against experimental data.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

In Z2 topological metals, nontrivial band topology and strong spin-orbit coupling (SOC) impose symmetry constraints that can stabilize unconventional superconducting states, even when thermodynamic probes indicate an isotropic gap. Here, we investigate the superconducting ground state of such a material, SrPd2As2, using muon spin rotation and relaxation (muSR), first-principles calculations, and Ginzburg-Landau analysis. Transverse-field muSR indicates a fully gapped superconducting state below Tc = 0.94 K, while zero-field muSR detects spontaneous internal magnetic fields below Tc, establishing time-reversal symmetry (TRS) breaking. Electronic structure calculations identify SrPd2As2 as a Z2 topological metal with surface states crossing the Fermi level. Standard anisotropic Migdal-Eliashberg calculations predict a nodal gap and overestimate Tc, indicating that a purely phonon-mediated pairing mechanism is insufficient. We resolve this apparent contradiction by showing that the interplay of SOC, tetragonal symmetry, and an open Fermi surface topology stabilizes a nonunitary triplet superconducting state whose symmetry-imposed nodes lie in momentum-space regions devoid of electronic states. This yields a fully gapped thermodynamic response while naturally breaking TRS. Our results establish SrPd2As2 as a clean platform for bulk nonunitary triplet pairing and a promising candidate for intrinsic topological superconductivity.

</details>

### [On Constructing and Decoding Quantum Triorthogonal Codes](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24519v1)

**Authors:** Alessio Baldelli, Olai Å. Mostad, Hsuan-Yin Lin, Eirik Rosnes, Massimo Battaglioni  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.24519v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.24519_figures/2605.24519_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Fig. 1. Subfigs. (a) and (b): LER of two triorthogonal codes on the dephasing channel for several decoders. Subfig. (c): Comparison of the estimated average number of binary operations per decoded frame for the J95, 1, 7K triorthogonal code for qGRAND (−106 and −107) and BP2+OSD-CS-60.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper provides a new mathematical framework for constructing triorthogonal quantum codes using integer linear programming, moving beyond traditional methods. It also demonstrates that a quantum-adapted version of the GRAND decoding algorithm offers superior performance and efficiency for these codes under dephasing noise.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the difficult construction and decoding of triorthogonal quantum CSS codes, which are essential for transversal T-gates in magic-state distillation.

**Main result.** The authors derive an existence criterion for triorthogonal matrices using MacWilliams identities and an Integer Linear Programming (ILP) formulation, discovering new triorthogonal codes and identifying an adapted qGRAND decoder as a highly promising decoding strategy.

**Method.** The study employs Integer Linear Programming (ILP) with symmetry reduction for code construction and evaluates various decoding algorithms, including Bounded-Distance Decoding, BP+OSD, and a quantum-adapted version of the GRAND algorithm.

**Model / system.** The work focuses on binary quantum CSS codes (specifically triorthogonal codes) subject to a memoryless dephasing (Z-type) error channel.

**Key observables.** Logical Error Rate (LER), dual minimum distance (d_perp), and computational complexity (binary operation counts).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Code length (n), dual distance (d_perp), dephasing probability (p), and OSD search depth (lambda).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study is limited to binary CSS codes and assumes a dephasing-only noise model; it also assumes error patterns in the GRAND decoder can be pre-loaded in memory.

**Figures summary.** Figures show the Logical Error Rate (LER) versus dephasing probability (p) for different triorthogonal codes, comparing the performance of BDD, BP+OSD, and qGRAND decoders, alongside a comparison of computational costs.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the triorthogonal code problem, presents an ILP-based construction framework using algebraic tools, details the discovery of new codes, evaluates decoding performance under dephasing noise, and provides a detailed complexity analysis of the proposed decoders.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

A triorthogonal code is a binary quantum Calderbank-Shor-Steane (CSS) code defined by a triorthogonal matrix. Triorthogonal codes are a key ingredient in magic-state distillation, since they allow for transversal $\mathsf{T}$ gates, a non-Clifford logical operation useful for achieving universal fault-tolerant quantum computation. Their construction is challenging because it must satisfy simultaneous pairwise and triple-wise overlap constraints, as well as row-weight requirements. In this work, we study the construction and decoding of triorthogonal codes with prescribed dual-distance properties. We derive an existence criterion for even-weight triorthogonal generator matrices with a target dual minimum distance. The criterion combines triorthogonality constraints with MacWilliams identities via Krawtchouk-polynomial conditions on the dual weight distribution, yielding an integer linear programming formulation for the construction problem. We find new nontrivial triorthogonal codes that are not necessarily generated by classical triply-even codes. The decoding performance of high-distance triorthogonal codes obtained via the doubling construction is then evaluated over the dephasing channel. We compare bounded-distance decoding, belief propagation plus ordered-statistics post-processing, and a GRAND-based decoder adapted to the quantum setting, which turns out to be a promising option.

</details>

**Authors:** Florian Krötz  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.25974v1>

PauLIB AoS and SoA (solid lines, lower band) are one to two orders of magnitude faster than PauliEngine and Qiskit (dashed lines, upper band), with all four lines following O(N2) scaling. At 500 qubits AoS outperforms SoA due to better cache locality.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** PauLIB is a high-performance C++ library designed to accelerate the manipulation of large Pauli sums. By using bit-packed symplectic representations and SIMD-optimized data layouts, it provides orders-of-magnitude speedups and much lower memory usage compared to standard Python-based quantum computing frameworks.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Processing large Pauli sums (millions of terms) is a major computational bottleneck in quantum chemistry and Pauli propagation due to Python overhead and inefficient data structures like hash maps.

**Main result.** The PauLIB library achieves massive speedups, including 660x faster single Pauli multiplication than Qiskit and up to 21,000x faster commutation grouping than PennyLane, while significantly reducing memory footprint.

**Method.** A C++20 header-only library using a bit-packed binary symplectic representation, a sorted array layout for SIMD-friendly operations, and both Array-of-Structures (AoS) and Struct-of-Arrays (SoA) memory layouts.

**Model / system.** The library focuses on the Pauli group, specifically handling large-scale Pauli strings and sums used in molecular Hamiltonians (via Jordan-Wigner or Bravyi-Kitaev transforms) and quantum error correction.

**Key observables.** Pauli string multiplication latency, Hamiltonian outer-product multiplication time, commutation grouping speed, and memory footprint (RSS).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Benchmarks performed at 500 qubits; includes comparisons of different rotation angles (Pauli, Clifford, and non-Clifford).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The current implementation is limited by the memory capacity of a single machine.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the AoS memory layout; Figure 2 shows the SoA layout; Figure 3 compares Pauli multiplication latency; Figure 4 shows Hamiltonian outer-product scaling; Figure 5 shows commutation grouping time; Figure 6 compares memory usage between PauLIB and Qiskit.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the computational bottleneck, details the three core architectural choices (symplectic representation, sorted array, and SoA/AoS layouts), presents performance benchmarks against existing frameworks (Qiskit, PennyLane, PauliEngine), and discusses future scaling and distributed-memory directions.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Processing large Pauli sums is a significant bottleneck in quantum chemistry, Pauli propagation, and Pauli-based compilation. Existing frameworks often suffer from Python interpreter overhead or utilize hash-map data structures that hinder SIMD vectorization and complicate multi-threaded merging. We present PauLIB, a header-only C++20 library designed to eliminate these bottlenecks through three key architectural choices. A bit-packed binary symplectic representation that encodes each qubit in two bits, reducing Pauli multiplication to a bitwise XOR and a population count; a sorted array layout that replaces hash maps to enable branch-predictable SIMD bulk operations; and a struct-of-arrays (SoA) memory layout that exposes contiguous word arrays for explicit SIMD vectorization. Benchmarks at 500 qubits show that single Pauli string multiplication runs at 25ns per operation-14 times faster than PauliEngine and 660 times faster than Qiskit-flat across all pair counts tested. Hamiltonian outer-product multiplication is approximately 10 times faster than PauliEngine and 45 times faster than Qiskit at all tested sizes. Greedy commutation grouping, the dominant preprocessing cost in variational algorithms, achieves up to 21,000 times speedup over PennyLane, driven by the compact bit-packed representation. The compact layout reduces the memory footprint of a one-million-term Hamiltonian at 500 qubits from 1,036MB (Qiskit) to 142MB, a 7.3 times reduction that directly enables larger problem sizes within a fixed memory budget. PauLIB is open source and provides C++ and Python interfaces.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.24103"></a>
### [Probing the limits of the semiclassical Einstein equation](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24103v1)

**Authors:** Gustavo Schranck Habermann, Daniel A. Turolla Vanzella  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.24103v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.24103_figures/2605.24103_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1: A particle with mass m (with GNm/ℓ≪1) in either 4-momentum state |pµ 1⟩[see (a)] or |pµ 2⟩[see (b)] — or any other momentum state |pµ⟩, with pµ belonging to the mass shell pµpµ = −m2 (dashed gray line) —, would engender only a weak gravitational field — according to any covariant criterion —, regardless how large the values of energy and 3- momentum are in a given frame. However, a system with 4-momentum ⟨pµ⟩= (pµ 1 +pµ 2)/2 = (γm, 0) [see (c)] can generate a strong gravitational field provided γ is large enough.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper proposes a method to test the limits of the semiclassical Einstein equation by using a superposition of boosted masses. It shows that the nonlinear nature of gravity can cause the semiclassical average to predict a strong-gravity regime that is absent in the individual quantum branches. This provides a way to potentially distinguish between quantum and semiclassical gravity using branch-degenerate observables.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating the validity and limits of the semiclassical Einstein equation, specifically whether the approximation holds when quantum fluctuations of the stress-energy tensor are large or when nonlinear gravity is involved.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that the semiclassical approximation can predict a strong-gravity, nonlinear regime even when individual branches of a quantum superposition are in a weak-field regime, due to the amplification of energy-momentum in the averaged stress-energy tensor.

**Method.** The study uses an analytical approach with the exact Levi-Civita metric in Weyl canonical form, applying Lorentz boosts to compare the predictions of a quantum superposition of states versus a semiclassical average.

**Model / system.** A toy model consisting of a cylinder with low proper linear density in a coherent superposition of two opposite relativistic boost velocities.

**Key observables.** The rate of change of the proper circumference with respect to the proper radius (dC/dr).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Dimensionless mass parameter (sigma), Lorentz factor (gamma), and the Tolman gravitational mass per unit length (Sigma).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The use of a 'flat-interior' approximation for the quantum scenario, which may break down in the semiclassical regime as the gravitational field becomes non-perturbative.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates the difference in gravitational content between the two scenarios.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of semiclassical gravity validity, defines a toy model of a boosted cylinder, performs analytical calculations for both quantum and semiclassical scenarios, and compares observable predictions to demonstrate the breakdown of the approximation.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

In the context of semiclassical gravity, the semiclassical Einstein equation is often invoked when backreaction of quantum matter/fields on the spacetime is at stake. It is expected to hold when quantum fluctuations are small. Yet, it is routinely used to justify the central role of the expectation value of the stress-energy tensor of quantum fields, whose fluctuations formally diverge. Here we propose a new way to probe the limits of this approximation by exploiting peculiar nonlinearities of gravity. As a proof of principle, we construct a controlled, analytically tractable setting where the incoherent mixture of weak-gravity states drives the system into a strong-gravity regime. By selecting a branch-degenerate observable, one can compare predictions of quantum and semiclassical gravity, potentially delimiting the validity of the latter.

</details>

**Authors:** Walid El Maouaki, Muhammad Shafique  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.25783v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Final test accuracy of FedAvg and Q-RAIL across linear, ring, and full entanglement patterns under increasing CX-fold values. Higher CX-fold amplifies circuit noise by repeating entangling operations.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper introduces Q-RAIL, a framework designed to make Quantum Federated Learning more robust to the noise and hardware differences found in NISQ devices. By weighting client updates based on the reliability of their specific hardware and circuit depth, it prevents noisy backends from degrading the global model. This approach significantly improves learning accuracy and stability in heterogeneous quantum networks.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Addressing hardware heterogeneity in Quantum Federated Learning (QFL), where varying noise levels and transpilation-induced circuit changes across different NISQ backends cause unreliable client updates.

**Main result.** The Q-RAIL framework significantly improves QFL accuracy (e.g., a 10.0-point gain on MNIST) and robustness against 'bad clients' by using a reliability-aware aggregation method.

**Method.** Q-RAIL computes a client-specific effective noise budget using backend calibration metadata and transpiled circuit statistics, then applies a stabilized, temperature-scaled softmax weighting for aggregation.

**Model / system.** The study uses Variational Quantum Classifiers (VQCs) with angle-encoding feature maps and various ansatz topologies (linear, ring, full) implemented on simulated heterogeneous NISQ hardware (Qiskit FakeProvider).

**Key observables.** Test accuracy, test loss, Area Under the Curve (AUC), and effective noise budget.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temperature scaling (tau), uniform mixing (beta), minimum weight floor (w_min), qubit counts (4, 10, 15), and CX-fold noise amplification.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes the server has access to backend calibration metadata and transpilation statistics; focuses on the NISQ era where noise-induced device shift is a primary driver of degradation.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the Q-RAIL workflow; Figure 2 ranks backends by error; Figure 3 details the methodology flowchart; Figures 4-6 show learning curves, robustness to bad clients, and scalability with client count; Figures 7-8 show heatmaps of noise budgets and accuracy relative to circuit complexity.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of hardware heterogeneity in QFL, proposes the Q-RAIL framework, details the reliability-scoring and aggregation mechanism, presents benchmarking results across various datasets and noise regimes, and discusses limitations and future directions.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum federated learning (QFL) on NISQ hardware is highly sensitive to backend heterogeneity: some clients contribute informative updates, while others contribute noise-dominated drift that uniform averaging cannot distinguish. We propose Q-RAIL (Quantum Reliability-Aware Federated Inference and Learning), a circuit- and calibration-aware aggregation method for hardware-heterogeneous QFL. Q-RAIL computes a client-specific effective noise budget from backend calibration metadata together with transpiled circuit statistics. This budget is converted into stabilized aggregation weights using temperature scaling, uniform mixing, and a minimum-weight floor. Q-RAIL was evaluated across multiple experimental settings, including an ablation study, and benchmarked against state-of-the-art methods on three datasets: MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, and OrganAMNIST. On the primary MNIST benchmark under strong hardware skew, Q-RAIL improves final test accuracy from FedAvg's 0.777 to 0.877, a +10.0-point gain corresponding to about 44.8% relative error reduction, while also exceeding the strongest wpQFL baseline (0.833). At the same time, test loss drops from 0.722 to 0.585, and test AUC rises from 0.920 to 0.973. Under non-IID MNIST, Q-RAIL reaches 0.813 vs 0.722 for FedAvg. It also outperforms FedAvg in 12/12 ansatz/CX-fold stress configurations and remains stronger at 4, 10, and 15 qubit setups. Overall, the results support calibration-driven, circuit-aware aggregation as a practical path toward robust QFL on heterogeneous quantum hardware.

</details>

### [QGCL: Quantum-Guided Clause Learning for Cryptanalytic SAT](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25756v1)

**Authors:** Walid El Maouaki, Alberto Marchisio, Muhammad Shafique  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.25756v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.25756_figures/2605.25756_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Fig. 1: Asymptotic scaling motivation for applying Grover search to sufficiently large extracted CNF subformulas with n Boolean variables and m clauses. The red curve represents the classical worst-case term 2n, while the green curve represents Grover-style subformula search with clause-oracle overhead, approximately m·2n/2. The crossover near n⋆≈2 log2 m marks the regime where the extracted conflict region is large enough for Grover guidance to become especially attractive. m is fixed to 200 clauses in this plot. TABLE I: Worst-case asymptotic cost of CNF-based side-channel checking with n Boolean variables and m clauses.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.25756_figures/2605.25756_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Fig. 4: Circuit sketch for SAT-oracle building blocks. (a) Clause-violation ancilla c1 for the clause C1 = (x1 ∨¬x2). X gates on the positive literal x1 retarget the control so that the multi-controlled X on c1 fires only when both literals in C1 are false (clause violated), i.e., when x1 = 0 and x2 = 1. Thus c1 = 1 encodes C1 violated. (b) Two such clause-violation bits c1, c2 are combined with a multi-controlled Toffoli to implement f ←¬c1 ∧¬c2, the CNF flag that is 1 iff no clause is violated. A controlled-Z on f applies the Grover phase to satisfying assignments. All ancillas are later uncomputed.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.25756_figures/2605.25756_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Fig. 6: Grover-subsolver output distributions for small CNF formulas used to validate the local oracle and sampling loop. For an extracted CNF with nsub variables and msub clauses, the oracle uses nsub + msub + 1 qubits. Panels (a,b) show ideal simulations: satisfiable cases amplify marked assignments, while unsatisfiable cases remain near-uniform. Panels (c,d) show noisy Fake Prague simulations, where the correct assignment is often still among the highest-probability candidates despite noise.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.25756_figures/2605.25756_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Fig. 7: Comparison of the CDCL baseline (blue) and the QGCL hybrid solver (orange) on the benchmark family with T ∈{2, 4, 8, 12}. Panels show (a) decisions, (b) propagations, (c) conflicts, and (d) restarts. Points denote individual runs; solid markers and error bars show the mean and 95% confidence interval across 10 seeds. Percentage labels indicate the relative improvement of QGCL over CDCL.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.25756_figures/2605.25756_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Fig. 9: QGCL performance when varying the Grover budget, defined as the maximum size of the extracted subformula (number of distinct variables plus clauses), on the same fixed instance used in the call-count sweep. Panels show (a) decisions, (b) propagations, (c) conflicts, and (d) restarts. The classical CDCL baseline mean and 95% confidence interval are plotted in blue; QGCL (hybrid) means and 95% confidence intervals appear in orange.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper proposes a hybrid algorithm called QGCL that integrates Grover search into classical SAT solvers to accelerate AES cryptanalysis. By using quantum search to provide local heuristic hints rather than attempting a global search, it significantly reduces the computational effort required to solve massive, structured search problems.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The difficulty of recovering AES-128 secret keys from noisy power side-channel leakage, which is a massive and highly structured combinatorial search problem impractical for full-scale Grover search.

**Main result.** The QGCL framework achieves up to an 86% reduction in solver conflicts and improves wall-time in ideal simulations by using Grover search to provide heuristic guidance to a classical CDCL solver.

**Method.** A hybrid quantum-classical approach (QGCL) where Grover search is invoked on small, dynamically extracted subformulas (conflict cores) to provide branching hints (variable activities and polarities) to a classical CDCL solver.

**Model / system.** The problem is modeled as a Boolean Satisfiability (SAT) problem in Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF) derived from AES-128 power side-channel leakage, simulated using the PennyLane framework on an NVIDIA GPU.

**Key observables.** Solver-internal statistics including conflicts, decisions, propagations, restarts, and total wall-time.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Grover budget (subproblem size), maximum number of Grover calls (C_max), mixing parameter (eta_t), and the depth of the cryptanalytic problem (T).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The effectiveness relies on a useful operating regime where quantum subproblems are small enough for near-term hardware; the framework assumes the quantum output only influences heuristics and cannot alter the logical SAT/UNSAT result.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows asymptotic complexity scaling; Figure 2 shows the AES side-channel workflow; Figure 3 illustrates the QGCL control flow; Figure 5 shows subformula extraction; Figure 6 compares ideal vs. noisy Grover outputs; Figure 7 compares CDCL vs. QGCL performance metrics.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the cryptanalytic challenge, defines the QGCL hybrid framework and circuit construction, details the subformula extraction and heuristic update methods, presents experimental results on various scales and noise levels, and concludes with future work.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Power side-channel attacks on AES exploit data-dependent physical leakage to recover secret keys, but turning noisy leakage observations into a verified AES-128 key remains a hard combinational search problem. SAT-assisted power side-channel cryptanalysis addresses this challenge by encoding AES semantics, key constraints, plaintext/ciphertext consistency, and leakage predicates as CNF, so that candidate keys must satisfy the exact cryptographic specification. These cryptanalytic SAT formulas are large and highly structured; our largest controlled AES-oriented power-SCA instances contain up to 39,389 variables and 137,712 clauses, making a full-formula Grover search well beyond the scale studied here and beyond currently practical near-term implementations. We propose QGCL, a Quantum-Guided Conflict-Driven Clause Learning (CDCL) framework in which Grover search is invoked only on small subformulas extracted dynamically around CDCL conflict cores. The quantum subsolver returns candidate assignments and violation scores that bias branching heuristics, while final SAT/UNSAT decisions and key verification remain classical. We evaluate QGCL on AES-oriented cryptanalytic SAT instances derived from power side-channel CNFs with leakage-derived hint configurations, measuring conflicts, restarts, decisions, and propagations. The experiments show consistent reductions in these solver-internal statistics on harder instances, with up to an 86% reduction in conflicts compared with the classical conflict-learning baseline. Parameter sweeps over the number of Grover oracle calls and the subproblem size identify a regime in which a modest quantum resource allocation captures most of the observed improvement.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.26090"></a>
### [Quantum Domain Decomposition for Preconditioning the Finite Element Method](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26090v1)

**Authors:** Elise Fressart, Michel Nowak, Nicole Spillane  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.26090v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.26090_figures/2605.26090_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Fig. 1: Example of 8 overlapping subdomains (d = 2, N1 = 4, N2 = 2, L = 3, and δ = 2−L).</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a quantum-compatible domain decomposition framework to precondition large-scale linear systems. By reducing the condition number of FEM-discretized PDEs, it enables more efficient execution of quantum linear solvers, potentially overcoming the scaling limits of current quantum algorithms.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Addressing the scalability bottleneck in Quantum Linear System Solvers (QLSS) by reducing the condition number of large-scale matrices arising from the Finite Element Method (FEM).

**Main result.** The authors prove the feasibility of quantum domain decomposition and provide upper bounds for the block-encoding parameters and computational complexity of the preconditioned system.

**Method.** The study utilizes a two-level Additive Schwarz preconditioner with BPX local solvers, implemented via block-encoding and Quantum Singular Value Transformation (QSVT) for the Poisson equation.

**Model / system.** The mathematical model is the Poisson equation (a diffusion-type PDE) on a d-dimensional hyperrectangle, discretized using the Finite Element Method (FEM) on a Cartesian mesh.

**Key observables.** The condition number of the preconditioned operator, the subnormalization factor (alpha), and the complexity of computing inner products (quantities of interest).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Mesh size (h or L), overlap parameter (delta), number of subdomains (N), and the diffusion tensor (rho).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The domain is assumed to be a hyperrectangle, the diffusion coefficient is constant over each mesh element, and the local solvers are implemented via block-encoding.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates the spatial arrangement of overlapping subdomains in a 2D setup; numerical tables compare condition numbers and eigenvalue ratios across different preconditioners and subdomain counts.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from the FEM discretization setting to domain decomposition and spectral bounds, followed by block-encoding construction, cost analysis, and a numerical proof-of-concept.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Even in cases where quantum linear solvers provide significant speedup compared to their classical counterparts, their performance depends on some of the same parameters. In particular, the condition number of the matrix which is to be inverted is a decisive parameter. A well known classical, and now quantum, remedy is to precondition the linear system $A x = b$ by premultiplying it by a matrix $H$ in such a way that the condition number of $HA$ is significantly smaller than the condition number of $A$.   In this work, we focus on a family of preconditioners called domain decomposition. First, we prove that it is feasible to apply quantum domain decomposition. We provide upper bounds for the block-encoding parameters of the Poisson problem discretized by the finite element method and preconditioned by the two-level Additive Schwarz preconditioner (one of the most fundamental domain decomposition techniques). From these bounds, we deduce the complexity of the quantum linear system solver. Second, we focus on a particular choice of local solver within the domain decomposition preconditioner by applying recent work by [Deiml and Peterseim, \textit{Math. Comput.}, 2025] on the Bramble--Pasciak--Xu (BPX) preconditioner. Finally, we provide details on how the operators are implemented.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

### [Quantum Parameterized Self-Attention Network for Image Classification](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25365v1)

**Authors:** Wenwei Zhang, Jintao Wang, Tianyu Ye, Changgeng Liao  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.25365v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Fig. 1. The VQA hybrid computational architecture: the quantum computer handles quantum state manipulation, while the classical computer performs parameter optimization and iterative updates.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.25365_figures/2605.25365_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Fig. 2. Overall architecture of QPSAN. The core distinction from classical ViT is the replacement of the dot-product operation with the QPA Scoring Module.</figcaption>
</figure>
